title: #product: 1768589014
preview:
Marissa Cole: trying to close the loop on cost model v1 scope for Q1. Console needs consistent breakdowns, Optimize needs what-if. can we align on MUST-have fields + endpoints for v1 vs v2?

Talia Benson: +1. I’d like a crisp list we can paste into PRD + release checklist. Also want to avoid "v1 is everything".

Aditya Rao: from Optimize side: we can work with a constrained what-if as long as the inputs are stable and we can compute ROI without hand-wavy assumptions. today we’re blocked by missing/ambiguous cache + quant knobs.

Chloe Martin: Console needs a deterministic breakdown that matches what we show in dashboards (prefill vs decode + overhead). Also we need bounded payload sizes for hot paths.

Jada Williams: + caching strategy matters. if the endpoint is called per-page load and returns 1000 rows of component detail we’re dead.

Ethan Park: ok, proposing we define v1 around 3 endpoints:
1) `POST /v1/cost/estimate` (per request estimate)
2) `POST /v1/cost/breakdown` (aggregate breakdown for a query window)
3) `POST /v1/cost/what-if` (projection based on workload summary + config deltas)

Ethan Park: and we keep it internal-only + versioned schema (so Console/Optimize can negotiate).

Marissa Cole: sounds right. can we write down what data Console needs vs what Optimize needs, and what goes into the common denominator?

Felix Schneider: Console view mock requires:
- total cost (usd)
- prefill cost, decode cost, overhead cost
- token counts (prefill tokens, decode tokens)
- caching attribution (cache hit savings)
- deployment mode (hosted/dedicated/private)
- model + version
- time window grouping (hour/day)

Felix Schneider: Optional/nice-to-have: queue time, retries, region.

Aditya Rao: Optimize requires same baseline but also needs:
- batch efficiency assumptions (effective batch size / utilization)
- quantization profile id(s) + expected throughput delta
- cache state assumptions (enabled/disabled, hit rate)
- ability to pass workload summary (p50/p95 input/output tokens, req/s, context length distribution)

Jonas Weber: and we’ve been asking for confidence intervals for projections, but we can punt if we at least get a "confidence" enum + reasons.

Laura Bennett: finance view: overhead allocation must be explicit. if we are going to show overhead, it needs to be labeled as allocated vs request-attributable. otherwise people will try to tie it to invoices line-by-line.

Marissa Cole: +1 on overhead semantics. support tickets are already "Console doesn’t match invoice".

Ethan Park: ok, let’s separate:
A) primitives that power everything (tokens, cache, batch, quant, overhead)
B) surfaces (Console breakdown tables vs Optimize what-if)

Talia Benson: can we do a hard line: v1 = must be correct + deterministic + bounded; v2 = richer modeling/uncertainty.

Chloe Martin: yes please. determinism matters for Console diffs. if a refresh changes component rounding we get bug reports.

Jada Williams: also request bounding: for breakdown queries, we need server-enforced limits (max groups, max time buckets). v1 should include that contract.

Ethan Park: proposal for v1 REQUIRED INPUT fields (shared schema):
- `org_id` / `project_id` (tenancy)
- `deployment_mode` (hosted|dedicated|private)
- `model_id`, `model_version`
- `region`
- `hardware_sku` (or runtime class)
- `quantization_profile_id` (or "none")
- `token_counts`: prefill_tokens, decode_tokens
- `cache`: cache_enabled + cache_hit_prefill_tokens (or equivalent "tokens_saved")
- `batch`: effective_batch_size (or batch_utilization)

Ethan Park: and REQUIRED OUTPUT fields:
- total_cost_usd
- breakdown_cost_usd: prefill, decode, cache_savings (negative), overhead_allocated
- echo of schema_version + cost_model_version

Felix Schneider: for Console, I need the breakdown to sum exactly to total. also please keep component names stable.

Aditya Rao: Optimize: can we have decode/prefill costs separate even when caching is on? i.e. cache savings shown as its own line item.

Ethan Park: yep. (prefill + decode + overhead + cache_savings) = total.

Laura Bennett: overhead_allocated needs a policy tag / "overhead_policy_version" so we can explain changes across months.

Talia Benson: is that v1 or v2?

Ethan Park: v1 can include a string field `overhead_policy_id` returned with the breakdown, no need for more.

Marissa Cole: ok, what’s explicitly NOT v1?

Jonas Weber: cache warm-rate parameter? (we keep getting asked "cache isn’t always warm")

Aditya Rao: yes, but if it’s not v1 we need a documented default. otherwise ROI gets "too optimistic".

Ethan Park: I’d like to keep out of v1:
- cache warm-rate / time-to-warm modeling
- confidence intervals with full numeric bounds
- per-kernel/per-layer cost components
- cross-region failover cost modeling
- per-customer bespoke overhead splits

Chloe Martin: also not v1: per-request detailed trace of every micro-stage. Console doesn’t need it and it’ll explode cardinality.

Felix Schneider: +1. keep v1 breakdown taxonomy coarse.

Aditya Rao: can we at least get v1 `what-if` to accept a workload summary + explicit assumptions:
- cache_hit_rate (single scalar) OR tokens_saved
- batch_size_target
- quant_profile_candidate

Aditya Rao: even if it’s "simple" it lets us stop hardcoding.

Ethan Park: ok, but we need guardrails. v1 what-if accepts a bounded "workload summary" (p50 input/output tokens, req/s, concurrency, and optional histogram buckets capped at N). no arbitrary arrays.

Jada Williams: and we should explicitly say `what-if` isn’t to be called on the request path. async/offline for Optimize.

Marissa Cole: for Console, do we even want to integrate what-if? not in v1.

Chloe Martin: agreed, Console v1 only uses estimate + breakdown.

Talia Benson: can we write a v1/v2 table?

Talia Benson: v1 (must ship Q1):
- endpoints: estimate, breakdown, what-if (workload summary)
- taxonomy: prefill, decode, cache_savings, overhead_allocated
- required ids: model_version, quant_profile_id, hardware_sku, region, deployment_mode
- bounded aggregation + deterministic rounding rules

Talia Benson: v2:
- cache warm-rate + time-based effects
- uncertainty bands + confidence explanations
- richer runtime attribution (queue time, retries, fallback region/model)
- expanded breakdown taxonomy if needed

Laura Bennett: plus v2: better reconciliation tooling for invoice questions (not changing billing, but a "why differs" view). but ok if that’s separate.

Marissa Cole: this is good. one more: do we require prefill/decode token splits v1? runtime team said there’s still instrumentation gaps.

Ethan Park: we should require it in the schema, but allow `unknown` in early rollout with a flag + degrade to "total_tokens" only. however Console really wants the split.

Chloe Martin: we can feature-flag the UI columns. if split is missing, show total only + banner.

Felix Schneider: yep. but the API must be explicit: either return both numbers or return a structured error / reason. not silent zeros.

Ethan Park: agreed. v1 response includes `completeness` status (e.g. COMPLETE / PARTIAL_PREFILL_DECODE / PARTIAL_CACHE). (string enum)

Aditya Rao: that helps Optimize too. we can block recommendations if completeness != COMPLETE.

Jonas Weber: + please document defaults for what-if assumptions in the API response (e.g. "assumed cache_hit_rate=0.0"). even if it’s v1.

Ethan Park: ok. we can echo the resolved assumptions in a `assumptions_summary` string field (human readable) for now.

Talia Benson: action items:
- Marissa + Aditya: update PRD scope section with v1 vs v2 table (today)
- Ethan: confirm v1 required fields + completeness enum with eng (tomorrow)
- Chloe/Jada/Felix: sanity check aggregation bounding needs (max groups/buckets) and post numbers here
- Laura: send the overhead policy language we should use in UI/tooltips

Marissa Cole: :thumbsup: I’ll draft the table and tag you all.

Aditya Rao: :+1: I’ll add the Optimize what-if required inputs + guardrails text.

Laura Bennett: will forward the overhead policy snippet + the "allocated vs attributable" wording.

Chloe Martin: I’ll drop proposed bounds. initial thought: max 50 groups, max 90 days, max 24 buckets/day depending on granularity.

Ethan Park: works. I’ll also make sure we call out "not billing system of record" everywhere.

Talia Benson: thanks all — goal is to lock this by end of week so ENG can stop thrashing.

title: #product: 1768512077
preview:
Marissa Cole: trying to lock down 2 things for the console onboarding nudges experiment:
1) placements (where these show)
2) what we count as a “completed” step for measurement (vs just click)

Proposed steps: Create API key → First request → First 100k tokens → View usage dashboard. Would love quick alignment so I can update the spec + send to eng.

Kenji Nakamura: +1. For placement, I’m thinking we keep it super contextual. “Create API key” nudge on the empty state / first-time home + also on the API Keys page header. “First request” on the API Keys success screen + in the quickstart block. “Usage dashboard” on the first request success toast / banner.

Paula Mendes: on measurement: please pls don’t define completion as “clicked CTA” 🙏 we should measure completion as the actual milestone event firing (key_created, request_succeeded, etc). Click is still useful as CTR leading indicator.

Dylan Brooks: yep. also we need a clean definition for “first request” so we don’t count auth failures etc. I’d use “first successful 200 response from inference endpoint” not “request_started”.

Nadia Rahman: agree. we already have event names in the activation funnel draft:
- `api_key_created`
- `inference_request_succeeded`
- `token_milestone_reached` with milestone=100000
- `usage_dashboard_viewed`

We can treat “completed step” as first time we see the relevant event for that user/org.

Marissa Cole: perfect. question: do we want to attribute completion to the nudge exposure, or just measure delta in completion rate for cohort?

Logan Wright: for experiment readout we should do cohort-level deltas (treatment vs holdout). Attribution per nudge is nice-to-have but messy.

Nadia Rahman: +1. We can still capture `nudge_impression` + `nudge_clicked` with properties like `nudge_id`, `placement`, `cta_target` so we can debug/follow funnels, but decision metric should be cohort deltas.

Kenji Nakamura: on placements, one note: if we put “Create API key” nudge on every page we’ll annoy people who already did it. Eligibility rules matter.

Marissa Cole: totally. Eligibility rules draft:
- show Create API key if no keys exist for org
- show First request if key exists AND no successful request yet
- show 100k tokens if first request done AND <100k tokens within 7d
- show Usage dashboard if first request done AND hasn’t viewed dashboard

Does that match how we can target?

Dylan Brooks: yes, but watch for “existing org invited user” case. new user joins an org that already has keys + usage. They shouldn’t see step 1. probably also shouldn’t see “First request” if org already has inference usage? depends on if you want per-user activation vs per-org activation.

Nikhil Sharma: for hosted API activation we’ve been using per-org in the funnel (signups create org). But invites are a thing. For this sprint, are we optimizing the self-serve funnel for new orgs only?

Marissa Cole: good catch. I’d prefer per-org for now to keep it aligned w activation metric definition. We can later add per-user “first request” if needed.

Paula Mendes: 👍 per-org is consistent w the activation KPI doc (first successful request + 100k tokens within 7 days of signup). just make sure events include `org_id` + `user_id` so we can slice later.

Logan Wright: +1, and define “signup time” as `org_created_at` for this funnel. also: completion windows. Are we treating “completed” as “ever” or “within 7 days of signup”? For readouts we should use within 7d.

Marissa Cole: agree: for measurement we use within 7d for activation milestones. For UX we can keep nudges around longer (but eligibility will naturally drop off when done).

Leila Farouk: from QA side: please define dismissal behavior in the spec. If I dismiss “Create API key” but I still haven’t created one, should it reappear later? If it never comes back we’ll hurt activation.

Kenji Nakamura: design intent: dismiss = snooze, not permanent. maybe 24h cooldown unless they navigate back to home?

Dylan Brooks: easiest technically: persist dismiss per `nudge_id` for 24h (local + server) and re-eligible after TTL if completion event still not seen. Can also have “Don’t show again” only for non-critical nudges.

Marissa Cole: ok decision: for Step 1 + Step 2 nudges, dismiss is a 24h snooze (no “don’t show again”). For Step 3/4, can include “don’t show again” since they’re less critical. everyone ok?

Paula Mendes: yes. just ensure `nudge_dismissed` has `dismiss_type` (snooze vs permanent) so we can interpret CTR.

Nadia Rahman: +1 and on “Enable streaming” (was floated as an optional nudge): completion should be detecting `stream=true` on a successful request at least once. But that’s optional; I wouldn’t include as a core onboarding step in this first framework.

Marissa Cole: cool, we’ll keep streaming as an iteration idea, not in the first checklist.

Kenji Nakamura: placements summary proposal (for spec):
- Home (new org empty state): checklist widget w top item CTA
- API Keys page: inline nudge if no key OR after key created, swap to “make first request”
- After first successful request: banner w link to usage dashboard + docs

Marissa Cole: ✅ works. Dylan/Nadia can you confirm what “usage_dashboard_viewed” means? is that existing?

Dylan Brooks: we have “console_page_view” with route; can alias it as `usage_dashboard_viewed` in analytics. Eng can emit explicit event too.

Nadia Rahman: better to emit explicit `usage_dashboard_viewed` so the funnel is stable even if routes change. include props: `entrypoint` (nudge vs nav), `dashboard_tab`.

Logan Wright: +1 explicit event. We got burned by route renames before.

Marissa Cole: done. Action items:
- I’ll update the framework doc w placements + completion definitions (completion = milestone event, not click)
- Dylan/Nadia: please add explicit `usage_dashboard_viewed` to event spec + confirm per-org semantics
- Kenji: update Figma notes re dismiss = 24h snooze for step 1/2

Thanks all :thumbsup:

Kenji Nakamura: :thumbsup:

Dylan Brooks: 👍 will follow up in ENG ticket.

Nadia Rahman: ack. I’ll add required props + dedupe note (first occurrence only) and post link here.

Paula Mendes: ty! and let’s make sure we log both impression + completion so we can debug “saw nudge but completion didn’t happen” cases.

Marissa Cole: yep. closing the loop w growth + analytics after this.

Dylan Brooks: 👍 will follow up in ENG ticket.

Paula Mendes: ty! and let’s make sure we log both impression + completion so we can debug “saw nudge but completion didn’t happen” cases.

Marissa Cole: yep. closing the loop w growth + analytics after this.

title: #product: 1768040207
preview:
Marissa Cole: Question for launch decision: do we allow *quality signals* from shadow experiments to trigger **automatic rollback** of a rollout/canary at v1, or advisory-only w/ human in the loop? This is coming up in beta calls. 

Paula Mendes: I’d strongly prefer advisory-only for launch. The rollout engine today auto-rollbacks on hard health checks (5xx, latency) w/ super clear semantics. Quality is probabilistic + can lag.

Dylan Brooks: +1. UX-wise we can do “Regression detected” banner + suggested actions + one-click rollback. But making it automatic seems like we’ll get paged for false positives.

Selene Huang: The biggest issue is statistical stability. Even with deterministic bucketing, you can get noisy deltas on small sample sizes or skewed traffic. If we auto-rollback we need hard requirements: min n, CI bounds, and cool-down.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: please no auto rollback on v1 quality. At least not enabled by default. We’ll be in the blast radius when scoring lags or the metrics pipeline drops a dimension and suddenly deltas look huge.

Jonas Weber: Also rubric versioning. If rubric updates mid-experiment and we compare apples to oranges, the delta may shift. We can pin per experiment but we’ll still see drift when people restart experiments.

Allison Grant: + scoring lag alerting isn’t perfect yet. If scoring lags 30-60 min, you could rollback based on stale data. Then it’s hard to debug after.

Marissa Cole: What are folks hearing from customers? One Dedicated prospect basically wants “quality gate” before canary goes to 100%.

Paula Mendes: We can support “quality gate” as a *blocker* in the rollout UI (e.g., requires manual approval step) without automating rollback. That still meets a lot of compliance expectations.

Sean Gallagher: + a manual approval step is audit-friendly. Auto rollback is fine for deterministic signals (error budget burn), but quality is subjective. We’ll need a clear audit log anyway.

Marcus Lin: Security/compliance angle: if the system automatically changes production routing based on model-graded evals, we need to document it as an automated decision system. That has implications for regulated customers + internal policy review.

Talia Benson: For planning: this maps to PM-1846 / ENG-5413. We need a crisp v1 posture we can document + hold line in beta.

Selene Huang: If we ever do auto rollback, I’d want it behind a separate “Auto action” toggle w/ explicit thresholds + minimum samples. Something like:
- Evaluate per metric bundle
- Only act if (delta worse) AND (95% CI excludes 0) AND (n >= N_min)
- Only on sustained window (e.g., 30 min) to avoid flapping

Dylan Brooks: UX proposal: default “Advisory” mode. Show: “Quality regression detected vs control (high confidence).” Provide buttons: `Pause rollout`, `Rollback`, `Ignore for 24h`.

Paula Mendes: + and we can add webhook / Slack alert integrations so teams can wire their own automation if they really want.

Marissa Cole: Concern: if we only do advisory, are we undermining the “guardrails” story? People want the system to protect them.

Sean Gallagher: Protect them by being reliable :) We can protect with hard guardrails like “don’t progress to next stage until minimum sample size and scoring freshness achieved.” That’s different from rollback.

Allison Grant: Yeah: “progression gates” are easier than rollback triggers. Gate can be: scoring lag < X, sample size ok, metrics computed. If not, rollout pauses.

Jonas Weber: For v1 metrics, some are brittle (format adherence can move due to verbosity changes even when correctness improves). Auto rollback would punish good changes.

Selene Huang: Exactly. Also multiple comparisons: if we ship 10 metrics, one will occasionally look bad by chance. If auto rollback triggers on any single metric we’ll roll back constantly.

Paula Mendes: So maybe define a single “Quality score” aggregate? But that gets hand-wavy.

Selene Huang: Aggregate helps but still needs calibration per customer domain. For v1, I’d rather keep metrics separate and let humans decide.

Marcus Lin: Another consideration: shadow scoring uses model-graded eval templates. If the grader model has an outage or behavior change, we could rollback production for the wrong reason.

Dylan Brooks: + we need to be careful in copy: “quality signals are directional, not ground truth.” If we auto-act, that messaging becomes inconsistent.

Marissa Cole: What about an internal-only auto rollback mode for dogfood? Just so we can learn.

Sean Gallagher: Internal only + guarded by feature flag is fine, but must be opt-in and have a kill switch. Also should not page oncall unless we’re confident. 

Allison Grant: If internal: log every decision w/ inputs (n, window, CI, rubric version, scoring lag) so we can postmortem.

Paula Mendes: I’m good with: v1 GA = advisory-only. Beta = advisory-only but with “pause progression” gates. Internal dogfood can test auto rollback behind `shadow_quality_auto_actions` flag.

Selene Huang: + we should define the minimum conditions for even surfacing a “high confidence regression” label. Otherwise every small wobble gets interpreted as real.

Jonas Weber: I’ll draft suggested thresholds for v1 metric bundle (refusal rate, format adherence, instruction-following rubric) w/ minimum sample sizes + CI computation assumptions.

Marissa Cole: Ok. I’ll update PM-1846 with decision proposal:
1) Launch = Advisory-only (no auto rollback)
2) Rollout *progression gate* optional (pause until scoring fresh + n>=min)
3) Internal-only auto rollback experiment behind flag, no customer exposure

Sean Gallagher: +1. Please include explicit note: “quality automation will not change user response path by default.”

Dylan Brooks: I’ll align Console UX with this: default advisory banner + actions, plus “Auto actions (experimental)” section hidden unless flag.

Marcus Lin: Add to docs: if customers want automation, they can consume the API + webhooks and implement in their CI/CD / rollout pipeline. That keeps responsibility clear.

Marissa Cole: Cool. I’ll also ping Priya for exec alignment.

Priya Natarajan: Reading. Agree with advisory-only for v1. Let’s not ship an auto-rollback feature we can’t defend w/ strong guarantees. Internal experiments ok. Please make sure we can tell a coherent story: “quality gates” = stop-the-line, not automatic reversions.

Marissa Cole: :thumbsup: will do. (reactions: Dylan Brooks :thumbsup:, Sean Gallagher :thumbsup:, Selene Huang :thumbsup:)

title: #product: 1767777777
preview:
Ethan Park: Quick sync - sales flagged 3 paying customers asking for fixes they say block adoption. Do we treat these as hotfix or next minor?
Marta Gomez: Context from sales: two customers (Enterprise A, BetaStart) report model truncation in their ingestion flow; one (Enterprise B) wants a new filtering dropdown (feature).
Priya Kapoor: Rule of thumb: if it blocks customer onboarding or breaks billing/ingest -> hotfix. UI preferences -> next wave unless high-severity for new users.
Alex Morgan: Technical check: truncation looks like tokenization mismatch introduced in last deploy. If root cause is config we can patch in <6 hours. That feels like hotfix material.
Tina Huang: QA note: if it's config-only we can run a quick regression suite. But if it touches tokenization library we'll need 24h for test matrix.
SalesOps-bot: Sales ticket links:  #1234, #1256, #1280. Priority: Enterprise A (P0), BetaStart (P1), Enterprise B (P2).
Ethan Park: Proposal: treat Enterprise A & BetaStart as hotfix-candidates. Enterprise B -> backlog under "next-wave/1.2". Agree?
Marta Gomez: Agree on candidates but want SLA promise window to Enterprise A. Can we commit to a timeline?
Alex Morgan: Tentative: triage now, if root cause is config -> ship same-day hotfix; if code change -> aim for next 48h hotfix window. Capacity: 2 small fixes or 1 medium.
Priya Kapoor: Additive criteria for hotfix (short):
- Customer revenue impact (paying, onboarding window)
- Severity: blocks key flow or causes data corruption
- Risk: low infra/DB impact preferred for quick patch
If 2/3 true -> hotfix.
Tina Huang: QA checklist for hotfix PRs (add to template): `tests: smoke, ingest-pipeline, tokenization-regressions, metrics-check`
QA-bot: QA checklist snippet saved to .github/PR_TEMPLATE:hotfix_checklist.
Ethan Park: Who owns comms to customers? Marta to draft a brief: incident description, ETA, workaround. We'll route through CSMs.
Marta Gomez: I'll send customer notes within 2 hours and tag CSMs.
Priya Kapoor: Milestone naming: `patch/1.0.2-hotfix` for urgent fixes; `wave/1.2` for enhancements. I'll create these and move issues accordingly.
Alex Morgan: Implementation note: if we do a hotfix branch, enable `canary=10%` and `monitor: error-rate, token-drop, latency`. Rollback tag: `hotfix/rollback-1.0.2`.
Ethan Park: Also, can we fast-track testing for config-only changes via a staged config roll? Use `feature-flags` to toggle and avoid full deploy if possible.
Alex Morgan: Good idea. We'll push config change behind `ff.token-fix` and flip during canary. Less risky.
Tina Huang: I'll prepare a shortened regression matrix for config toggles: 6 core flows + 2 customer-specific test cases. That should be runnable in ~90 minutes.
Priya Kapoor: Decision: Enterprise A & BetaStart -> hotfix-candidates. Enterprise B -> next-wave. Owners: Alex (eng lead), Tina (QA), Marta (customer comms), Priya (product).
SalesOps-bot: Noted. Updating CRM with ETA fields.
Ethan Park: Final ask: collect post-hotfix acceptance criteria so we can close the customer ticket. Prefer "no token truncation for 24h on canary + confirmation from CSM".
Marta Gomez: Will capture acceptance criteria in the tickets.
Alex Morgan: No blockers from eng; will start triage now.
Tina Huang: QA ready for accelerated run.
Priya Kapoor: Thanks team. We'll do a 30-min check-in at 15:30 PT and lock the hotfix scope then. :thumbsup:

title: #product: 1767123456
preview:
Maya Li: Quick mid-q check: merchant-routing milestone — what shipped this sprint, what slipped, and immediate next steps? Owners please reply with one-line status + blockers. (cc @here)
Ethan Park: shipped: basic routing config rollout to prod (v1.2.0) :tada:. Slipped: dynamic fallback policy (PR in review) due to perf regression. Next: finish perf fix + CR by EOD Wed. @Carlos can you help with regression trace?
Carlos Mendez: on it. I saw increased p99 on cudnn kernel — bisecting now. ping @eng-infra if we need to revert quantized kernel.
Zara Khan: blocked on infra autoscale tuning for dedicated pools — current policy overshoots mem by ~15%. Owner: Jo? Next: I can run a canary with lower burst cap if infra gives green light.
Jo Grant: I can run canary today after Ethan's PR lands. Small ask: need the canary config in this gist: https://gist.github.com/redwood/abc123
Ethan Park: PR: https://github.com/redwood/merch-routing/pull/462 — fixed regression, added unit test. @Maya can you prioritize CR?
Maya Li: I'll review after stand. Quick decision: if perf still high after Carlos' bisect, fallback to v1.1.9 is approved. Anyone object? :+1: / :-1:
Jo Grant: :+1: prefer gradual canary > full rollback.
Carlos Mendez: bisect: culprit seems to be fused attention kernel on 2nd stage. Working on patch. ETA ~6 hrs.
deploy-bot: deploy success: merch-routing v1.2.0 -> prod at 2025-08-02T14:32:10Z (sha: 9f4d8c7) :rocket:
Ethan Park: Noted. Will queue canary config and attach to PR.
Zara Khan: Dependency note: product analytics needs new telemetry to validate fallback behavior. @Maya can you loop them in?
Maya Li: Looping analytics and CS now (pinged in #customer-success). Also adding to milestone doc: https://docs.redwood.ai/okr/q3/merchant-routing
Jo Grant: Thanks — I'll coordinate canary window: 6–8pm PT. Volunteers to monitor?
Carlos Mendez: I can monitor infra metrics. @Ethan can you push a smoke test script?
Ethan Park: script attached: ```./scripts/smoke.sh --endpoint /routing/check --qps 5 --duration 10m``` 
Zara Khan: Great. I'll validate telemetry after smoke. If all green, we'll mark 'on-track' in OKR.
Maya Li: Thanks everyone. I'll update OKR sheet after canary. Short summary to paste into doc: shipped: rollout; slipped: dynamic fallback (in PR); blockers: perf regression on fused kernel; next: patch + canary tonight; owners: Ethan (code), Carlos (patch), Jo (canary), Zara (telemetry).

title: #product: 1765554321
preview:
Alex Chen: Quick proposal — for Q3 OKRs I want to consolidate embeddings + rerank into a single 'Retrieval' slice to reduce shipping overhead. Key asks: one rollout, unified telemetry, shared caching. Acceptance criteria I’m proposing:
1) end-to-end p95 latency < 250ms for typical 1k-token rerank workloads
2) embeddings 99th < 60ms for small-vector calls
3) regression delta < 1% on our 10 seed customer prompt sets
4) canary + instant fallback to previous model/version.
Thoughts? tagging @eng-runtime @sales

Maya Patel: I like consolidation for developer velocity, but worried about a cost spike for high-throughput rerank. We should bake in cost guardrails: autoscale ceilings + a soft-degrade (approx-match fallback) when cost thresholds hit.

Samir Rao: +1 to cost guards. Also need per-route batching hints and a required quantization plan. Suggest we require a quant profile and a benchmark script in the RFC/PR.

Jordan Blake: From Sales: three enterprise customers ask for per-tenant logs and an SLA on rerank. That pushes toward offering a dedicated-capacity routing option for those tenants — so we can’t do a single-hosted-only rollout unless we support per-tenant routing.

ProductBot: Automated note: RFC placeholder created — https://redwood.internal/rfcs/673 — reviewers: @eng-runtime, @product.

Alex Chen: OK — counter-proposal: do a phased rollout. Phase A: unified API surface + telemetry + opt-in per-customer dedicated routing (so enterprises can pin). Phase B: cost/autoscale optimizations, cache/kv sharing, and single unified backend. Phase A launch criteria: telemetry live, canary run with 10% traffic, 1 enterprise onboarded to opt-in routing, and no regression >1% on eval set.

Maya Patel: Phase A sounds reasonable if we enforce concurrency caps and budget alerts on the canary. Add an ops checklist (OOM monitoring, eviction policy, cache hit dashboards).

Samir Rao: I'll wire up the benchmark harness and include sample runs in the RFC. Will add a required `bench/emb-rerank.sh` that emits p95/p99 and cost-per-req numbers. Canary config snippet I’ll add to PR:
```
canary:
  percent: 10
  route: rerank-v2
  fallback: rerank-v1
```

Jordan Blake: Sales OK with phased approach but needs a timeline for enterprise onboarding — target: enterprise onboarding within 6 weeks after Phase A. Also request: simple docs for POST params and per-tenant logging toggle.

Alex Chen: Great — I’ll author the RFC with the phased acceptance criteria and ping @eng-runtime and @sales next week. TL;DR: consolidate slices for Q3, ship phased rollout, include cost & SLA guards. :thumbsup:

ProductBot: Reminder: Weekly roadmap sync Thu 10:00am — add this RFC to the agenda.

Alex Chen: Great — I’ll author the RFC with the phased acceptance criteria and ping @eng-runtime and @sales next week. TL;DR: consolidate slices for Q3, ship phased rollout, include cost & SLA guards. :thumbsup:

ProductBot: Reminder: Weekly roadmap sync Thu 10:00am — add this RFC to the agenda.

title: #product: 1763652104
preview:
Aditya Rao: Need a quick gut check on MVP scope for Optimize quality gates before I lock PRD copy. Two open q’s:
1) which config changes must run FULL gates vs FAST smoke gates?
2) how should overrides look in Console (and what do we allow at MVP)?

Paula Mendes: +1. Also from Rollouts side we need a crisp rule we can encode (block canary promotion unless gate status == passed or overridden).

Marissa Cole: Let’s anchor on “safe-by-default” but don’t accidentally make Optimize unusable for low-risk changes.

Jonas Weber: From evals POV: batching + cache settings usually change latency/throughput, but can still change outputs in edge cases (timeouts, truncation, different batching order, etc). Quantization is *def* higher risk.

Selene Huang: Agree. We’ve seen quality deltas on quant profile switches, especially long context. I’d vote: quant profile changes => always full gate.

Aditya Rao: Proposed MVP rule (draft):
- batching policy update (max batch tokens, dynamic batching window): smoke gate required, full gate optional based on “risk score”
- prefix/KV caching: smoke gate required, full gate if toggling caching on/off or changing eviction policy
- quantization profile change: full gate required (smoke gate as precheck)
Thoughts?

Dylan Brooks: For Console UX, I really need a deterministic “why is Apply disabled” message. Risk score is fine but we need to explain it in plain English.

Kenji Nakamura: +1. If we do “risk score”, it needs to translate into clear labels like “Low risk (smoke gate)” vs “High risk (full gate)”. Avoid numbers.

Paula Mendes: Also: rollout coupling. We can do:
- allow canary start if smoke passed
- allow canary promotion to 100% only if full passed (or exception)
But PM decision: do we require full gate *before* any canary for quant? I’d prefer yes.

Jonas Weber: If quantization change is scoped to only a tiny % canary, still want full gate first. Otherwise we’re using prod traffic as eval. :grimacing:

Selene Huang: +1 full before canary for quant. For batching/cache I’m ok with canary after smoke if the smoke suite includes the “hard cases” slice.

Aditya Rao: OK, so: quant profile switches: full gate pre-req to enable canary.
Batching/cache: smoke gate pre-req to enable canary; full gate required for promoting to 100%? or only when classified high risk.

Marissa Cole: MVP should keep policy simple. My suggestion:
- For MVP: batching/cache => smoke gate required for apply + canary enable. Full gate required only for “tier-1 routes” (enterprise + high volume) OR when change includes toggles (cache on/off) vs parameter tweaks.
- For MVP: quant => full gate always.
We can refine with risk score later.

Dylan Brooks: If policy depends on route tier, we need Console to show the policy source (“Enterprise policy requires full gate”). That’s doable.

Paula Mendes: That helps Rollouts. We already have “route tier” and “account tier”. We can encode a default gating policy per tier.

Kenji Nakamura: UX question on overrides:
- are overrides a “request” flow (someone else approves)
- or “I acknowledge risk” self-serve?
We should pick one for MVP.

Aditya Rao: I’m leaning request/approve (time-bound) for MVP even if it’s slightly slower. We have audit trail requirement.

Ava Chen: Jumping in — after that near-miss last quarter we shouldn’t ship self-serve “I accept risk” for anything that changes outputs. Override must be explicit owner + expiration + reason. Ideally 2-person rule for enterprise routes.

Marissa Cole: +1. For MVP we can do a single approver role (oncall/PM) but must be *separate* from the requestor.

Dylan Brooks: From Console implementation: we can support states:
- Failed (Apply blocked)
- Override requested (blocked, pending)
- Override granted (Apply enabled until expiration)
We can show a banner on the Optimize rec card.

Kenji Nakamura: Copy suggestions:
- “Quality gate failed: regression above threshold”
- “Override required to apply this change”
- “Override expires in X hours”
Also needs a “View evaluation details” link.

Jonas Weber: Override request form should require selecting which evidence you have. At minimum: “reviewed artifacts”, “impact limited to canary X%”, “rollback plan”. Even if we don’t enforce, capture it.

Selene Huang: +1. Also require choosing a reason code so we can track: false positive, urgent incident, customer request, eval harness outage.

Aditya Rao: MVP override requirements (draft):
- fields: reason code, freeform justification, link(s) to gate run artifacts (auto attached), requested duration (max 24h), rollout scope (canary %)
- approver: designated “Optimize Gate Approver” role
- audit log always
- no override for “no baseline” cases (block hard)
Sound right?

Paula Mendes: +1 on “no baseline => no override”. Rollouts can’t safely proceed.

Dylan Brooks: For UI: we can display the artifacts link + diff summary + comparison to baseline. For the reason code, dropdown + text area.

Kenji Nakamura: Please add explicit “This may change model outputs” warning modal on final confirm. Not scary, just clear.

Ava Chen: And default duration should be short (4h?) with max 24h. Force renewal via new approval.

Marissa Cole: Agree. Also: in MVP, for batching/cache low-risk, allow override but still require approval for enterprise routes.

Aditya Rao: OK. Proposed MVP policy to capture:
- Quant profile change => full gate required pre-canary; overrides allowed only w/ approver + expiry, but not allowed if baseline missing.
- Cache toggle (on/off) or eviction policy change => full gate required for enterprise + tier-1 routes; smoke gate required otherwise; canary allowed after smoke for non-tier-1.
- Batching parameter tweaks => smoke gate required; full gate only for enterprise/tier-1 promotion.
- Overrides always require approver (not self-serve), with reason code + justification + expiry.
I’ll update PRD + send for sign-off.

Jonas Weber: Works. Please tag me when you update; I’ll ensure smoke suite coverage includes long-context + tool-calling prompts.

Dylan Brooks: Works for Console. Just confirm we’ll have an API field that returns: gate_required_level (smoke/full) and gate_status + override_status.

Paula Mendes: Cool. I’ll align Rollouts behavior to: cannot promote unless (full passed) OR (override granted + not expired).

Kenji Nakamura: I’ll draft the modal/warning copy + the override request UI states and send screenshots.

Aditya Rao: Thanks all. :thumbsup: I’ll post PRD diff in this thread later today.

Marissa Cole: Please also add a “What happens if gate times out?” sentence. We need deterministic behavior (treat as fail, allow retry).

Aditya Rao: good catch — will add: timeout => failed w/ retry option; no apply unless passed/override.

Ava Chen: And make sure audit log captures *who approved* and *why*. Security will ask.

Aditya Rao: yep.

title: #product: 1761234999
preview:
Marta Ruiz: Kicking off a short thread to capture decisions from the routing dials deep-dive. Goal: crisp launch criteria + customer promise for multi-region + tenant isolation. Keep replies short, add owners.
Liam O'Connor: Thinking in 'dials' helps: latency-first, cost-first, residency-first. Each dial maps to a policy template and a cost estimate.
Priya Nair: Implementation note: templates translate to routing rules + scheduler hints. We need explicit model variant mapping (quantized vs fp16) per template.
Ethan Park: SRE concern: how do we surface capacity headroom per-region? Need a headroom metric and a hard-fail path. Also define evacuation behavior when headroom < 5%.
Sofia Gomez: Sales feedback: customers want two things: predictable pricing line item for "isolation" and a one-liner SLA we can show prospects. We need an agreed phrase and price bucket.
Nora Blake: Legal: any "hard isolation" promise should come with a minimum commitment and auditability clause. Also flag data residency certification wording for EU customers.
Marta Ruiz: OK can we agree on minimal launch artifacts? I'll propose: 1) Policy templates doc, 2) Acceptance checklist, 3) Pricing stub, 4) Runbook + evacuation playbook.
Priya Nair: Add: migration tooling spec that defines: traffic-staging, cache-warm, eval-gate, cutover window. Simple example YAML sketch:
```
migration:
  tenant: T-987
  template: residency-first
  canary_pct: 5
  warm_kv: true
  eval_suite: "core-promptset-v2"
  rollback_triggers:
    p95_increase_pct: 20
    quality_regression_pct: 3
```
Liam O'Connor: For policy templates: recommend these three initial templates: latency-first, shared-economy, residency-first (hard/soft variants). Each template lists precedence: tenant override > region rule > org default.
Ethan Park: Evacuation options matrix (ops):
- overflow -> spill to shared pool (best-effort)
- queue -> if request is non-blocking, allow queued window 30s
- reject -> if strict isolation requested -> reject with 429 + guidance URL
We should map template -> evacuation default.
Sofia Gomez: Pricing stub: residency-first (soft) +10% vs baseline; residency-first (hard) +35%; latency-first +5%. These are rough - finance to confirm.
Nora Blake: Add compliance flag: "auditable-path" must be true for hard isolation. Also require KMS key escrow option.
Marta Ruiz: Acceptance checklist draft (short):
- Canary: X% for 24-72h (template-specific)
- Telemetry: per-tenant token cost, p50/p95, error rate, cache hit-rate
- Quality: eval suite pass or rollback
- Capacity: headroom >= 10% before full cutover
- Audit: migration log + who/why field present
Liam O'Connor: Automation owner: Priya for migration tooling; I'll take policy template DSL and cost estimation model.
Priya Nair: I'll scaffold the migration flow in a spike: api + CLI + console hooks. CLI example:
```
redwood migrate start --tenant T-987 --template residency-first --canary 5 --notify on
redwood migrate status --id migrate-20240301-01
```
Ethan Park: SRE will draft evacuation playbook and define headroom metric. Will include alert thresholds and automated action suggestions.
Sofia Gomez: I'll prepare a short sales-facing FAQ: definitions (soft vs hard isolation), expected lead time, pricing tiers.
Nora Blake: I'll draft the audit clause and minimum-term language for the contract stub.
ci-bot: Note: RFC doc template created at https://go.redwood/docs/routing-dials-rfc (please review).
Marta Ruiz: Thanks everyone. Action items: Priya -> migration spike; Liam -> policy DSL + precedence table; Ethan -> evacuation playbook; Sofia -> pricing FAQ; Nora -> contract clauses. Will collect updates in the RFC and schedule a follow-up next week.

title: #product: 1759923310
preview:
Talia Benson: hey folks — quick alignment request for the status page transparency upgrades. before we lock the launch plan, can we agree on (a) launch criteria and (b) measurable outcomes? want something concrete for PM-620 + the internal announcement.

Marissa Cole: +1. also helpful for Console integration timing — we need a crisp “what ships in phase 1 vs phase 2” so we don’t block on UX polish.

Paula Mendes: agree. comms/templates are getting close, but “done” keeps moving because we don’t have explicit gates.

Rafael Mendes: ok, propose we define criteria per rollout phase:
- Phase 0 (internal dogfood): taxonomy + mapping in place, incident-bot drafting working, and on-call can actually use it without extra steps
- Phase 1 (limited public rollout): components visible + templates live + manual posting workflow solid
- Phase 2 (full): automation + audits + post-incident summaries policy + Console links

Allison Grant: for measurable outcomes, we should pick metrics we can actually collect without heroics:
1) TTFU (time to first public update)
2) status accuracy (component/region/capability)
3) reduction in support escalations / tickets disputing status
4) anti-flap rate (status toggles per incident)

Dev Patel: yes pls on #3. we can tag disputed-status tickets (like “status-accuracy”) and baseline current weekly volume. if this works, we should see fewer “status page says ok but X is failing” tickets.

Talia Benson: great. can we put numbers to these? otherwise it’ll read hand-wavy.

Rafael Mendes: TTFU target: for Sev0/Sev1, first status update within 15 min of incident declaration. for Sev2, within 30 min. (not a public SLA, internal goal.)

Paula Mendes: + include “if still investigating, say that” guidance so we don’t wait on root cause. templates already reflect that.

Allison Grant: accuracy: we can measure via the weekly audit job comparing incident tags vs status updates. target could be: 95%+ of incidents have correct component + region within the first 30 min of posting. and 99%+ correct by incident resolution.

Marissa Cole: question: what’s “correct” mean in partial impact? e.g., streaming only in us-east for some routes. do we require capability+region always?

Rafael Mendes: for Phase 1, require component + region, capability when applicable (Streaming vs API vs Embeddings/Reranking). the intake form will enforce required fields. if uncertain, we can mark broader initially but we must narrow within 60 min once we confirm.

Dev Patel: from customer POV, broad is still better than missing, but it’s frustrating when it stays broad the whole incident. so yes: “broad then narrow” rule is good.

Elliot Price: for launch criteria, can we add “docs updated” as a gate? we’ll need a short public explainer of what the new components mean + how to interpret ‘degraded performance’ vs ‘partial outage.’ otherwise Support will keep answering the same questions.

Talia Benson: +1. so launch criteria (Phase 1) includes docs + Support macros refreshed.

Marissa Cole: phased rollout suggestion:
- Week 6: internal dogfood only (status page visible changes behind feature flag / staged component list)
- limited public rollout to Hosted API components only (no Dedicated/Private yet), 1–2 regions to start (us-east + eu-west)
- then expand to all key regions and add Console linking once stable

Allison Grant: on anti-flap: we can measure “component state changes within 30 min” — target <2 toggles per component per incident (excluding initial investigating→identified etc). mostly we want to avoid degraded/operational bouncing.

Paula Mendes: comms quality gate: templates in incident-bot must produce draft text that passes “no sensitive info” checks (no customer names, no capacity details, no vendor blame). we can add a short checklist in the runbook.

Rafael Mendes: launch criteria checklist draft:
1) component taxonomy finalized + ownership map approved
2) mapping registry validated + schema checks in CI
3) dashboards exist per component/region (links from runbook)
4) incident-bot intake requires component/region (+ capability where relevant)
5) status update templates live (investigating/identified/mitigating/monitoring/resolved)
6) comms roles (IC/Comms Lead/Scribe) documented + training done
7) support escalation playbook + macros updated
8) measurement in place: TTFU, accuracy audit, support ticket tags

Talia Benson: perfect. i’ll capture as:
- Launch criteria = checklist above
- Success metrics (first 30 days):
  - median TTFU Sev0/1 <= 15m; Sev2 <= 30m
  - accuracy audit pass rate >=95% at 30m; >=99% by resolution
  - disputed-status tickets down 30% vs baseline
  - anti-flap: <2 unexpected toggles/component/incident
- Rollout: Phase 0 internal → Phase 1 limited Hosted API (us-east/eu-west) → Phase 2 full regions + automation + Console links

Marissa Cole: sounds good. note: Console links should not show Dedicated/Private incident details unless incident is already public. we’ll keep that constraint explicit in the phase plan.

Dev Patel: works for Support. pls share the baseline window you’re using for ticket volume so we’re comparing apples to apples.

Talia Benson: yup — i’ll do baseline = last 8 weeks (pre-rollout). will post in PM-620 and link the dashboard once Allison confirms the tag query.

Allison Grant: i’ll get you the query + confirm audit job output fields today.

Paula Mendes: i’ll update the training agenda to include “broad then narrow” + examples of acceptable language.

Talia Benson: awesome. i’ll send a recap in Linear + drop the criteria into the launch checklist doc.

Allison Grant: on anti-flap: we can measure “component state changes within 30 min” — target <2 toggles per component per incident (excluding initial investigating→identified etc). mostly we want to avoid degraded/operational bouncing.

Paula Mendes: comms quality gate: templates in incident-bot must produce draft text that passes “no sensitive info” checks (no customer names, no capacity details, no vendor blame). we can add a short checklist in the runbook.

Rafael Mendes: launch criteria checklist draft:
1) component taxonomy finalized + ownership map approved
2) mapping registry validated + schema checks in CI
3) dashboards exist per component/region (links from runbook)
4) incident-bot intake requires component/region (+ capability where relevant)
5) status update templates live (investigating/identified/mitigating/monitoring/resolved)
6) comms roles (IC/Comms Lead/Scribe) documented + training done
7) support escalation playbook + macros updated
8) measurement in place: TTFU, accuracy audit, support ticket tags

Talia Benson: perfect. i’ll capture as:
- Launch criteria = checklist above
- Success metrics (first 30 days):
  - median TTFU Sev0/1 <= 15m; Sev2 <= 30m
  - accuracy audit pass rate >=95% at 30m; >=99% by resolution
  - disputed-status tickets down 30% vs baseline
  - anti-flap: <2 unexpected toggles/component/incident
- Rollout: Phase 0 internal → Phase 1 limited Hosted API (us-east/eu-west) → Phase 2 full regions + automation + Console links

Marissa Cole: sounds good. note: Console links should not show Dedicated/Private incident details unless incident is already public. we’ll keep that constraint explicit in the phase plan.

Dev Patel: works for Support. pls share the baseline window you’re using for ticket volume so we’re comparing apples to apples.

Talia Benson: yup — i’ll do baseline = last 8 weeks (pre-rollout). will post in PM-620 and link the dashboard once Allison confirms the tag query.

Allison Grant: i’ll get you the query + confirm audit job output fields today.

Paula Mendes: i’ll update the training agenda to include “broad then narrow” + examples of acceptable language.

Talia Benson: awesome. i’ll send a recap in Linear + drop the criteria into the launch checklist doc.

title: #product: 1753004821
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads-up — planning to expose the new "Insights (beta)" panels in Console behind a hidden flag. Need a quick plan for canary audiences + guardrails.
Raghu Patel: +1. Which flag key are we using? Is this a per-account exposure or role-based?
Ava Chen: flag key = console.beta.insights_ui. We want role-based first — product and analytics teams + 5% of DAU orgs as canary. then expand to 25% if no regressions.
Monica Li: For telemetry, we'll want event batching on insights_load and a QA prompt set. Can we gate telemetry collection until users opt-in to beta so we don't skew metrics?
Kai O'Neill: Suggest: a two-tier rollout — roles: [product, analytics, se-early] + random org hash bucket for pct. Guardrail rules:
```yaml
console.beta.insights_ui:
  scope: org
  roles: [product, analytics, se-early]
  rollout_pct: 5
  metrics: [insights_load, insights_error_rate, latency_p50]
  auto_increase_to: 25
```
Raghu Patel: That looks good. Who will toggle the flag? We need an audit log entry and a rollback playbook.
Ava Chen: I can flip in ff-admin for canary after the PR merges. Need @devex to confirm the ff-admin UI path and owners.
devex-bot: Hi! ff-admin path is /internal/flags. Only users with role `flag-admin` can toggle. All changes are logged to /audit/flags.
Monica Li: QA checklist:
- smoke load the insights panel
- check 95th pct latency under 300ms
- no console errors in Sentry for that org
- verify telemetry events have beta=true
Raghu Patel: PR #4923 ready for review — @Ava can you ping reviewers? Also would like staging toggled first and run the QA set for 24h.
Ava Chen: Pinging reviewers now. Staging toggle request submitted to deploy-bot.
deploy-bot: Flag console.beta.insights_ui toggled to true in staging for org=all (by request from Ava Chen).
Kai O'Neill: After 24h of staging, if metrics stable, we'll roll to 5% prod via random bucket and add a notice in console header for beta users. Also add a banner and feedback link.
Ava Chen: Sounds good. I'll coordinate comms with @Monica and @Raghu for the feedback form and customer support heads. Please leave any objections here by EOD.
Raghu Patel: No objections. :thumbsup:
Monica Li: No blockers from QA side.
Ava Chen: Great — merging in the next hour. I'll post a brief update in #product when canary is live.

title: #product: 1749604442
preview:
Henry Cho: quick q before we lock scope for benchmarks launch — do we want to surface the benchmark results inside Console for logged-in users too (in addition to the public landing page)? I’m getting a couple pings from CS + a few internal folks who want it “in-product.”

Henry Cho: PRD for the interactive page is here for context: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/PROD/Interactive+Benchmark+Page+Requirements (see section on disclaimers + export + analytics)

Soojin Lee: from PMM side: I’d love the *option* eventually, but I’m worried about mixing “marketing report” and “product metric” semantics. The report is a point-in-time snapshot w/ strict caveats. In Console it’ll read like a live SLO dashboard.

Nikhil Sharma: +1. also we don’t want people to assume the numbers reflect *their* org / keys / routing policies. Hosted API performance varies with params + region + load + cached prefixes.

Felix Schneider: implementation-wise, we can render the same JSON dataset in Console pretty quickly once ENG-18566 (results service/cache) is stable. But the UX needs to make it very obvious it’s the same published dataset, not live telemetry.

Alicia Perez: if we put it in Console, we need a dedicated info block / tooltip pattern for disclaimers. Otherwise it will look like “Redwood Console performance tab.” That’s a different bar.

Logan Wright: also analytics: if we move it in-product, the events need to be distinct from marketing-site events (filters/export/CTA). Otherwise we’ll blow up attribution. Tyler — thoughts?

Tyler Benson: yeah, mixing funnel metrics w/ product events is messy. If we do it, we should use separate event names + properties, and *not* count Console views as “landing page visits” for campaign reporting.

Soojin Lee: what’s the user story? Is it “I’m already a customer and want to compare regions/models” vs “I’m evaluating Redwood and need proof”?

Henry Cho: hearing two asks:
1) SEs want to pull it up during logged-in demos without leaving Console.
2) CS wants a canonical in-app link when customers ask “where did these numbers come from?”

Wei Chen: for #2, we can solve via a contextual link to the public methodology + report from Console (e.g., help panel). That’s low risk and doesn’t create “live metrics” confusion.

Felix Schneider: we can do a simple Console module: “Benchmark Report (public)” with an embedded iframe or a link-out. But iframe on app.redwoodinference.com might be weird w/ CSP + auth.

Nikhil Sharma: link-out is safer. If we embed, we own perf + caching + outages. Remember launch week cache miss storm risk — we don’t want that incident to take down Console UI.

Alicia Perez: design preference: small card in Console > “Inference economics benchmark” + 2 bullets + “View interactive report” link. No tables in Console for v1.

Soojin Lee: agree. keep the interactive table experience as the canonical surface for now.

Michael Grant: legal angle: the published benchmark has specific required disclaimers and an “as-of date.” If we render inside Console, it needs the same disclaimers, same version/date, and no implication of ongoing monitoring. Otherwise it’s a different claim.

Henry Cho: makes sense. what about “gated” inside Console for logged-in only? any benefit?

Michael Grant: gating doesn’t reduce claim risk if it’s still used in sales or by customers. It’s still a representation by the company.

Logan Wright: from platform side: also ensure any in-product surface doesn’t accidentally expose the raw artifacts endpoint that’s intended for the marketing site (auth/caching assumptions differ).

Felix Schneider: ok so proposal:
- v1: add Console card w/ link to public landing page + methodology (and maybe PDF download). No embedded dataset.
- post-launch: revisit “benchmarks in Console” as a separate PRD w/ clear distinction from live perf telemetry + separate analytics + version pinning.

Soojin Lee: +1. also let’s not create a new “Benchmarks” nav item this late.

Henry Cho: 👍 I’ll update PM-826 notes and mark “Console embed” as out-of-scope for launch. We’ll add a small link card to Console help/resources.

Tyler Benson: if you do the card, please route clicks through the same UTM / campaign tag conventions so we can see the path, but as a *separate* source dimension.

Wei Chen: I can draft the copy for the Console card + disclaimer snippet and run through legal review.

Michael Grant: works. send copy before end of week and include the version/date language.

Henry Cho: action items:
- Henry: update PM-826 + create follow-up ticket for “Benchmarks in Console (post-launch)”
- Wei: draft Console card copy + link destinations
- Tyler/Logan: confirm event naming for “console_benchmark_report_click” or similar

Soojin Lee: thanks all. keep launch clean. :shipit:

Felix Schneider: 👍 I’ll also flag to ENG that we should not assume Console consumes the benchmark JSON endpoint in v1.

Henry Cho: closing the loop here once I update the ticket.

title: #product: 1748761155
preview:
Lauren Bishop: trying to close semantics for “predictive mode” vs reactive scaling before we freeze PRD copy + Console strings. Can we align on precedence + what happens when forecast disagrees w/ saturation?

Lauren Bishop: specifically:
1) Is predictive “an extra signal” or “primary desired, with reactive overrides”?
2) When reactive says scale up NOW, do we ignore forecast?
3) How do we log/communicate “why” to customers (auditability)?

Vanessa Ortiz: my preference: treat forecast as a *bias* / floor on desired (ahead of time), but reactive is still the safety net.
Vanessa Ortiz: in other words: desired = max(reactive_desired, predictive_desired) for scale-up path, then clamp to bounds. For scale-down, be more conservative.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1 on max() for scale up. For scale down: min(reactive, predictive) is risky if predictor is wrong / missing.
Keiko Yamamoto: we should probably never let predictive drive scale-down by itself in v1. use reactive scale-down w/ cooldowns + min bound.

Ethan Park: from control plane POV we need something deterministic we can describe in API docs. I like: compute candidates, then apply a single arbitration function.
Ethan Park: also need explicit config field semantics: is predictive_mode = “enabled” and predictive_strategy = {scale_up_only}? or just implied.

Jordan Lee: we’ve been drafting ADR language like:
- predictive provides `requested_desired` (may be null)
- reactive provides `requested_desired`
- policy engine outputs `bounded_desired`
Jordan Lee: but product needs a crisp rule.

Mina Haddad: routing needs to know when we’re “bounded at max” and still saturated, so we can degrade gracefully (fallback model/region) without fighting autoscaler.
Mina Haddad: so whatever rule we pick, we need clear “max bound hit + queue rising” signal even if predictive is on.

Rafael Mendes: +1. from SRE, safety invariant is: prediction must never mask a real incident.
Rafael Mendes: if queue depth / saturation is high, reactive must be able to override (within max) and we page on “maxed + unhealthy.”

Tessa Morgan: UX question: should Console copy say “Predictive scaling (recommended)” vs “Predictive scaling (beta)” and explicitly note “Reactive scaling still protects latency”?
Tessa Morgan: customers will assume predictive replaces reactive unless we’re explicit.

Lauren Bishop: ok so proposal forming: predictive only influences scale-up (pre-warm), reactive always runs, reactive can force scale-up immediately.
Lauren Bishop: but do we want predictive to ever reduce scale-up (i.e., reactive wants +10, predictive says +2)?

Vanessa Ortiz: no, never reduce. reactive is responding to actual load; if it says +10, we should respect that. predictor being “low” can’t be trusted.
Vanessa Ortiz: else you get queue buildup and we’ll have to explain why we ignored real signals.

Keiko Yamamoto: agreed. plus we already have anti-oscillation & cooldown; letting predictor fight reactive will cause flapping.

Jordan Lee: so the arbitration could be:
- if predictive_enabled and forecast_fresh: predictive_desired computed
- reactive_desired computed
- unbounded_desired_up = max(reactive_desired, predictive_desired) (treat null as 0)
- unbounded_desired_down = reactive_desired (scale-down only)
- then choose between up/down based on current vs desired w/ cooldown logic
- finally clamp: bounded = min(max(unbounded, min_bound), max_bound)

Ethan Park: careful: “treat null as 0” is fine as an internal default but we should explicitly record “predictive unavailable” and why (stale, error, insufficient history).
Ethan Park: also, scale-down “only reactive” should be part of public semantics, otherwise customers will ask why it’s not “symmetric.”

Allison Grant: observability side, we can expose:
- requested_desired_reactive
- requested_desired_predictive
- desired_unbounded
- desired_bounded
- bound_hit{min|max}
- prediction_staleness_seconds
Allison Grant: that should cover “why didn’t it scale” and “why did it scale.”

Ravi Menon: question: does reactive have any “panic mode” beyond normal desired? e.g., if queue depth crosses critical, we should bypass cooldown.
Ravi Menon: else a bad forecast + cooldown could trap us.

Keiko Yamamoto: we have emergency scale-up path planned (ignore scale-up cooldown when hard saturation threshold exceeded). Still respects max bound.
Keiko Yamamoto: but we should align on which thresholds trigger it (queue depth, GPU util, tail latency).

Rafael Mendes: yes, and we need to document this as an invariant: “reactive overrides cooldown on scale-up under sustained saturation.”
Rafael Mendes: for scale-down, cooldown always applies.

Lauren Bishop: open question: if customer sets max bound too low and we’re saturated, do we ever auto-disable predictive or change behavior?

Vanessa Ortiz: no auto-disable. predictive isn’t the problem. we should surface “max bound hit” state + recommendations.
Vanessa Ortiz: plus routing can do its thing once it sees bounded + saturation.

Mina Haddad: exactly. if max is too low, routing should enter bounded-degraded policy (fallback model, shed low-priority traffic, etc.)

Tessa Morgan: UX: we can show a banner in deployment settings: “At max capacity. Requests may queue. Consider raising max or enabling fallback.”
Tessa Morgan: but we should avoid wording that implies we’ll exceed max.

Ethan Park: API semantics: bounds are hard. never exceed. if region shortage prevents meeting min, we need a separate “min_unmet” degraded state (already in ENG-22151).

Lauren Bishop: ok summarizing decision proposal (tell me if anyone disagrees):
- Predictive mode = proactive *scale-up bias*, not a replacement.
- Scale-up desired = max(reactive_desired, predictive_desired) when forecast fresh; otherwise predictive ignored.
- Predictive does not drive scale-down in v1.
- Reactive can bypass scale-up cooldown under “panic” saturation thresholds; still respects max.
- Final desired always clamped to [min,max].
- We log/emit requested vs bounded + reason codes (predictive_stale, bound_hit_max, panic_scale_up, cooldown_blocked, etc.)

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: +1

Keiko Yamamoto: +1

Rafael Mendes: +1 as long as “panic” thresholds are concrete and alerts page on max-bound + queue growth.

Allison Grant: works. I’ll update dashboard spec to include reason code breakdown.

Jordan Lee: I’ll incorporate into ADR draft as the precedence ordering + determinism requirements.

Ethan Park: I’ll make sure schema wording matches: “predictive_mode: scale_up_only” (or equivalent) so we don’t overpromise.

Tessa Morgan: I’ll tweak Console microcopy to explicitly say “Predictive helps pre-warm capacity; reactive scaling remains active.”

Ravi Menon: can we add one more explicit note: if prediction is higher than reactive, we still scale up (within max) even if queue is low, to reduce cold-start at known ramps.
Ravi Menon: that’s basically the value prop.

Lauren Bishop: yes, that’s the main point. I’ll update PRD language + add FAQ: “Why did we scale up even though utilization is low?” -> “based on forecast; see predictive desired + confidence.”

Vanessa Ortiz: we should also document forecast freshness window (e.g., ignore if >15m old) so behavior isn’t confusing.

Jordan Lee: ack. We’ll implement stale cutoff and record it in decision log.

Lauren Bishop: cool. I’ll post this summary in the PRD + link ADR once landed. thanks all.

- Final desired always clamped to [min,max].
- We log/emit requested vs bounded + reason codes (predictive_stale, bound_hit_max, panic_scale_up, cooldown_blocked, etc.)

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: +1

Keiko Yamamoto: +1

Rafael Mendes: +1 as long as “panic” thresholds are concrete and alerts page on max-bound + queue growth.

Allison Grant: works. I’ll update dashboard spec to include reason code breakdown.

Jordan Lee: I’ll incorporate into ADR draft as the precedence ordering + determinism requirements.

Ethan Park: I’ll make sure schema wording matches: “predictive_mode: scale_up_only” (or equivalent) so we don’t overpromise.

Tessa Morgan: I’ll tweak Console microcopy to explicitly say “Predictive helps pre-warm capacity; reactive scaling remains active.”

Ravi Menon: can we add one more explicit note: if prediction is higher than reactive, we still scale up (within max) even if queue is low, to reduce cold-start at known ramps.
Ravi Menon: that’s basically the value prop.

Lauren Bishop: yes, that’s the main point. I’ll update PRD language + add FAQ: “Why did we scale up even though utilization is low?” -> “based on forecast; see predictive desired + confidence.”

Vanessa Ortiz: we should also document forecast freshness window (e.g., ignore if >15m old) so behavior isn’t confusing.

Jordan Lee: ack. We’ll implement stale cutoff and record it in decision log.

Lauren Bishop: cool. I’ll post this summary in the PRD + link ADR once landed. thanks all.

Maya Brooks: Quick async check-in for the quarter's milestone — want to capture what actually shipped, what slipped, and immediate deps. TL;DR: runtime-v2 canary is live, quant-tuning work slipped into next sprint, and we have two blocking infra PRs.

```

- quant-tuning: Ravi + Priya (evals -> fix -> re-run)
- dedicated autoscale rollout: Liam (merge PRs + ops validation)
```

title: #product: 1748129999
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick design question for the compact Autoscale row: should min/max steppers compress into a single control on narrow widths? Sketch: https://www.figma.com/file/xyz/compact-autoscale
Diego Rivera: If we compress, we must preserve a11y labels for each value. Two hidden labels isn't ideal. Maybe a single dual-thumb input? :thinking:
Sam Ortiz: Dual-thumb is neat but might be unfamiliar. Another option: collapsed mode shows `Min 1 | Max 8` and a chevron to expand to steppers. Keeps visual simplicity.
Priya Sharma: UX note: when collapsed, show a small delta badge that says `+2 burst` if burst is enabled. Users scan that faster than numbers. Also include an affordance to edit inline with keyboard (Enter opens steppers).
Ava Chen: Good call. Accessibility: how do we announce the collapse state to screen readers? Suggest `aria-expanded` on the chevron and an offscreen summary: `Autoscale: min 1, max 8, burst allowed`.
accessibility-bot: Reminder: slider and steppers need descriptive names and live region updates for changes. Example: `aria-live="polite"` when estimate numbers change.
Diego Rivera: Copy nit for guardrail tooltip: current "SLO guardrail enforces latency targets" -> propose "Guardrail: prevents autoscale settings that push P95 above target". More actionable.
Sam Ortiz: Also include estimated impact line in tooltip: `Est. p95: 320ms · Cost: +8%`. If space is tight show `320ms · +8%` and reveal full explanation on hover/focus.
Priya Sharma: Edge case: pinned GPU pools should show a disabled min stepper with the reason. Suggested inline text: `Min locked by pinned pool` — make sure color isn't the only signal (add lock icon + `aria-disabled`).
Ava Chen: Visual detail: use a thin separator between min/max in collapsed view, and ensure touch targets stay >=44px. For the chevron, use a filled focus ring and a 3:1 contrast border.
Sam Ortiz: Interaction: when a user types a number into a collapsed inline editor, validate against guardrails and show inline toast: `Target violates guardrail: increases P95 above 450ms` with an action `Autoset to safe value`.
accessibility-bot: Keyboard checklist: Tab order should hit collapsed row, chevron (Enter/Space to expand), min input, max input, burst toggle. Tooltips must be reachable via keyboard and closable with Escape.
Ava Chen: I'll update the mock to show collapsed, expanded, and pinned-pool states. Will add aria-live snippets and the concise estimates. Tagging @Sam to review copy and @Priya for a11y signoff. ETA: tomorrow morning. :rocket:

title: #product: 1747532100
preview:
Ava Chen: quick design ping — new autoscale mock with a few novel bits: 'min floor' concept, a separate 'burst cap' slider, and a hold-to-apply on the SLO slider to reduce accidental high commits. want copy + accessibility eyes.
Noah Kim: Interesting. "Min floor" language could be confusing with existing "min" field. Maybe show it as "Min (guaranteed baseline)" and keep shorthand label.
Priya Shah: UX concern: hold-to-apply might be annoying for power users. Maybe show a confirmation pill only if change > 20% from current value.
Sofi Gomez: a11y: NVDA reads the new "burst cap" slider as just "slider" unless we add aria-label and aria-valuetext. Also ensure the hold-to-apply action is keyboard activatable (Enter after focus) and that assistive tech hears the pending state.
Marco Rivera: Copy idea for burst helper: "Burst cap: maximum temporary GPUs allowed during spikes. Not permanent; billing only for minutes used." Could also include an info link.
Ellie Tran: Fallback messaging needs to call out routing AND perf impact. Proposed toast: 
```
Fallback engaged — Selected pool exhausted. Traffic routed to Redwood Hosted to maintain availability. Expect higher tail latency; data stays encrypted. Reserve Dedicated capacity to prevent routing. (Learn more)
```
Ava Chen: Good. Also thinking of adding a tiny inline badge near min that reads "min floor active" when min > 0 and burst is off, with tooltip: "Guaranteed baseline capacity to avoid cold starts".
Noah Kim: Visual suggestion: show a faded overlay on the slider track for the burst cap zone so it's clear which part of the slider only applies to short bursts.
Priya Shah: For copy concision: "Burst cap: temporary extra GPUs. Auto-removes after spike." Or even shorter for tiny helper: "Temp extra GPUs — auto-remove."
Sofi Gomez: Keyboard flows: when opening pool selector, focus should move into the dialog and trap; closing returns focus. Also add an aria-live polite region for the cost delta when slider changes so screen reader users hear estimated impact.
Marco Rivera: Cost delta phrasing: show compact: "Est. +$0.12/hr" and detailed hover: "Estimated $0.12/hr based on current traffic profile. Methodology: average token throughput * model unit cost."
Ellie Tran: UX edge-case: if user pins a specific pool but also enables fallback, show a small warning: "Pinned pool may route off-cluster when capacity low." Maybe a toggle to disable fallback.
Ava Chen: Nice — we should surface a 'disable fallback' switch but warn about availability impact. Also add an undo toast for accidental disabling.
ui-bot: note: autoscale-experiment-2024 preview deployed to staging at 2024-12-03T21:10Z. Please test hold-to-apply, nvda, and cost live region.
Noah Kim: Action items: Priya will run two microcopy lengths for burst + min floor; Marco will mock overlay for burst cap and cost delta compact; Sofi will run NVDA + keyboard pass and add aria snippets; Ellie will draft fallback disable flow and undo toast. Target: staged QA by end of week.
Priya Shah: Will post microcopy variants later today.
Marco Rivera: I'll add the faded overlay and the compact est. cost next.
Sofi Gomez: I'll start NVDA testing tomorrow morning and drop exact aria attributes in Figma.
Ellie Tran: I'll write the fallback disable confirmation and undo copy.
Ava Chen: thanks all — will update figma with the hold-to-apply prototype and share the link here when ready. :rocket:

title: #product: 1747161850
preview:
Vivek Kulkarni: Need quick alignment here so we can lock messaging + scope. What do we mean by “supported” for Private observability minimum pack vs “premium”? Right now docs are fuzzy and Support keeps asking what they’re on the hook for.

Allison Grant: +1. We should define it in terms of (a) shipped artifacts, (b) default config we test/validate, (c) what Support/SRE will troubleshoot vs best-effort.

Neha Kapoor: From SRE POV: “supported” should mean if customer installs with our Helm values (or minimally modified) we can rely on dashboards + alerts being accurate and not exploding their Prometheus. If they swap out Prometheus/Grafana entirely (Datadog, etc) we can’t guarantee.

Priya Natarajan: Agree. We need crisp: supported == validated in reference envs + upgrade safe + documented runbooks. Anything else is “integrates with” not “supported”.

Logan Wright: For telemetry stack pieces: minimum pack includes 1) our metrics endpoints + recording rules, 2) curated alert rules, 3) dashboards bundle, 4) log collector config + exporters. Premium would be longer retention, federation, advanced tracing, etc.

Vivek Kulkarni: Ok. Let’s list what’s IN “minimum supported” (v1) vs explicitly deferred.

Elliot Price: Also need a single sentence we can put in docs + launch brief. Something like: “Redwood supports the default Prometheus/Grafana-based observability bundle shipped with the Private installer; alternative stacks are supported on a best-effort basis via documented export integrations.”

Dev Patel: + please include what Support will ask for on escalations. Today it’s random. If “supported” means we can ask for the baseline dashboards + rules to be installed and we can reproduce with the smoke test harness, that’s good.

Rafael Mendes: From on-call readiness: alerts need runbook links + severity mapping. “Supported” should include that alert annotations point to actions and don’t page on noise by default.

Markus Klein: Customer perspective: they’ll accept “supported minimum” if we’re clear on boundaries. Common ask: “Can I export to Splunk/ELK?” They don’t need us to run Splunk, they need a known-good config + what auth methods we support.

Vivek Kulkarni: Proposed definition (please poke holes):
- Supported minimum pack (GA target):
  1) Prometheus scrape configs for all Redwood Private components (control plane + runtime) + bounded-label metrics (no unbounded model/tenant/route labels)
  2) Recording rules for golden signals (RPS, error rate, latency quantiles, saturation/queueing)
  3) Prometheus alert rules with Sev mapping + runbook URLs in annotations
  4) Grafana dashboards (runtime overview, control plane health, infra/GPU panels)
  5) Structured logs from Redwood components + default collector config (batching/backpressure) with redaction guidance
  6) Export integrations supported/documented: Loki, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Splunk HEC (and optional OTLP logs if low-effort)
  7) Packaging for air-gapped installs (dashboards/rules/collector images in offline bundle) + checksums
  8) Post-install validation + smoke tests (scrapes up, dashboards render, sample alert fires)

- Out of scope for v1 / premium:
  a) Multi-cluster federation / central observability plane
  b) Long retention (>14d) / managed storage sizing beyond guidance
  c) Full distributed tracing with sampling controls + trace UI
  d) Custom dashboards/panels per-customer (we’ll take requests, not included)
  e) SIEM “solutioning” beyond the supported exporters

Neha Kapoor: Looks right. Key is #1: bounded labels. I want the statement explicit: we will not ship per-request labels; we aggregate. If customers want per-tenant breakdown, premium / custom.

Allison Grant: +1 and we should codify “supported labels” list. Also: include a note that if customers change scrape intervals / retention / resource limits we can’t guarantee performance, but we can provide recommended ranges.

Logan Wright: On exporters: I’d call OTLP “experimental” unless we test it end-to-end in both VPC + on-prem. Loki/ELK/Splunk are the ones we’re actively validating.

Vivek Kulkarni: Makes sense. So doc wording: “Supported sinks: Splunk HEC, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Loki.” “OTLP: optional/preview.”

Dev Patel: For supportability, please also include: “If you disable the collector, Support may ask you to re-enable for troubleshooting.” Otherwise we spend cycles on unknown logging setups.

Elliot Price: We should be careful with tone. Customers hate “re-enable it” demands. Maybe: “To receive full Support coverage for log-related issues, Redwood may request enabling the default log collector configuration.”

Priya Natarajan: +1. Also, we should say “supported” includes upgrade/rollback not breaking dashboards/alerts. This is an explicit commitment.

Rafael Mendes: Can we define “premium” as a future SKU? Or just “future enhancements”? Product messaging needs to be consistent.

Vivek Kulkarni: For now: “minimum supported” vs “advanced/premium (future)”. Not committing to SKU pricing in this thread.

Markus Klein: Another nuance: some enterprises already have Prometheus + Grafana. If they point our dashboards/rules to their existing stack, is that “supported”?

Neha Kapoor: I’d say yes IF:
- Prometheus >= our minimum version
- They deploy our ServiceMonitors/PodMonitors (or equivalent)
- They keep our rule groups intact
But we won’t debug their whole observability cluster. We debug Redwood targets.

Allison Grant: Recommend we phrase as: supported with “customer-managed Prometheus/Grafana” provided it meets documented prerequisites. Anything outside that: best effort.

Logan Wright: + include a compatibility matrix (Prometheus/Grafana versions) in the docs.

Vivek Kulkarni: Good. Action items:
1) Elliot: update docs/PRD language with this definition + boundaries.
2) Neha/Rafael: add Support coverage bullets + what we’ll ask for in escalations.
3) Logan/Allison: finalize “supported sinks” list + OTLP stance for v1.

Elliot Price: Ack. Will draft a “Supported configurations” section + a “Not included in minimum pack” callout.

Dev Patel: Can we also add a quick internal blurb for Support macros? E.g. “Minimum pack supported = dashboards present, alert rules loaded, collector running (or supported external sink), Prometheus scrape healthy.”

Rafael Mendes: I’ll write the checklist for Support and Oncall. Also adding runbook-link requirement to alert templates.

Priya Natarajan: One more: Installer preflight. If “supported” means turnkey, then failed prerequisites should be caught early (PVC storage class, network policies blocking scrapes, etc.). Let’s ensure we call those “hard-blocking” vs “warn-only” clearly.

Vivek Kulkarni: Yep. I’ll sync with Diego/Connor on installer checks, but for this thread: “supported” includes preflight + post-install validations.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: Also please add a statement: we don’t log request payloads by default; only metadata fields, with redaction guidance. Security will ask.

Vivek Kulkarni: Good call. I’ll incorporate: “No prompt/completion bodies in operational logs by default; audit logs handled separately.”

Markus Klein: Thanks. This will help a lot with customer conversations. They keep asking ‘are these dashboards officially supported or just examples’.

Neha Kapoor: If we do this, Support can finally say “yes, those are the ones” :)

Vivek Kulkarni: Cool. I’ll post a summary in the PRD and link back here.

Dev Patel: :white_check_mark: Please tag me on the doc so we can update the enablement kit.

Elliot Price: Will do. ETA tomorrow EOD.

Priya Natarajan: Closing thought: make sure “minimum supported” != “minimum viable”. It should feel production-ready. So we should keep the list small but real.

Vivek Kulkarni: Agreed. Keeping it focused on highest-impact failure modes + predictable troubleshooting.
