  b) Long retention (>14d) / managed storage sizing beyond guidance
  c) Full distributed tracing with sampling controls + trace UI
  d) Custom dashboards/panels per-customer (we’ll take requests, not included)
  e) SIEM “solutioning” beyond the supported exporters

Neha Kapoor: Looks right. Key is #1: bounded labels. I want the statement explicit: we will not ship per-request labels; we aggregate. If customers want per-tenant breakdown, premium / custom.

Allison Grant: +1 and we should codify “supported labels” list. Also: include a note that if customers change scrape intervals / retention / resource limits we can’t guarantee performance, but we can provide recommended ranges.

Logan Wright: On exporters: I’d call OTLP “experimental” unless we test it end-to-end in both VPC + on-prem. Loki/ELK/Splunk are the ones we’re actively validating.

Vivek Kulkarni: Makes sense. So doc wording: “Supported sinks: Splunk HEC, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Loki.” “OTLP: optional/preview.”

Dev Patel: For supportability, please also include: “If you disable the collector, Support may ask you to re-enable for troubleshooting.” Otherwise we spend cycles on unknown logging setups.

Elliot Price: We should be careful with tone. Customers hate “re-enable it” demands. Maybe: “To receive full Support coverage for log-related issues, Redwood may request enabling the default log collector configuration.”

Priya Natarajan: +1. Also, we should say “supported” includes upgrade/rollback not breaking dashboards/alerts. This is an explicit commitment.

Rafael Mendes: Can we define “premium” as a future SKU? Or just “future enhancements”? Product messaging needs to be consistent.

Vivek Kulkarni: For now: “minimum supported” vs “advanced/premium (future)”. Not committing to SKU pricing in this thread.

Markus Klein: Another nuance: some enterprises already have Prometheus + Grafana. If they point our dashboards/rules to their existing stack, is that “supported”?

Neha Kapoor: I’d say yes IF:
- Prometheus >= our minimum version
- They deploy our ServiceMonitors/PodMonitors (or equivalent)
- They keep our rule groups intact
But we won’t debug their whole observability cluster. We debug Redwood targets.

Allison Grant: Recommend we phrase as: supported with “customer-managed Prometheus/Grafana” provided it meets documented prerequisites. Anything outside that: best effort.

Logan Wright: + include a compatibility matrix (Prometheus/Grafana versions) in the docs.

Vivek Kulkarni: Good. Action items:
1) Elliot: update docs/PRD language with this definition + boundaries.
2) Neha/Rafael: add Support coverage bullets + what we’ll ask for in escalations.
3) Logan/Allison: finalize “supported sinks” list + OTLP stance for v1.

Elliot Price: Ack. Will draft a “Supported configurations” section + a “Not included in minimum pack” callout.

Dev Patel: Can we also add a quick internal blurb for Support macros? E.g. “Minimum pack supported = dashboards present, alert rules loaded, collector running (or supported external sink), Prometheus scrape healthy.”

Rafael Mendes: I’ll write the checklist for Support and Oncall. Also adding runbook-link requirement to alert templates.

Priya Natarajan: One more: Installer preflight. If “supported” means turnkey, then failed prerequisites should be caught early (PVC storage class, network policies blocking scrapes, etc.). Let’s ensure we call those “hard-blocking” vs “warn-only” clearly.

Vivek Kulkarni: Yep. I’ll sync with Diego/Connor on installer checks, but for this thread: “supported” includes preflight + post-install validations.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: Also please add a statement: we don’t log request payloads by default; only metadata fields, with redaction guidance. Security will ask.

Vivek Kulkarni: Good call. I’ll incorporate: “No prompt/completion bodies in operational logs by default; audit logs handled separately.”

Markus Klein: Thanks. This will help a lot with customer conversations. They keep asking ‘are these dashboards officially supported or just examples’.

Neha Kapoor: If we do this, Support can finally say “yes, those are the ones” :)

Vivek Kulkarni: Cool. I’ll post a summary in the PRD and link back here.

Dev Patel: :white_check_mark: Please tag me on the doc so we can update the enablement kit.

Elliot Price: Will do. ETA tomorrow EOD.

Priya Natarajan: Closing thought: make sure “minimum supported” != “minimum viable”. It should feel production-ready. So we should keep the list small but real.

Vivek Kulkarni: Agreed. Keeping it focused on highest-impact failure modes + predictable troubleshooting.

title: #product: 1746210905
preview:
Nikhil Sharma: Trying to lock “intent signals v1” today so Logan can finish the schema + we can stop bikeshedding in PRD. What *counts* as intent (docs views / pricing clicks / security page views) vs vanity? Need something we can defend to Sales + Privacy.

Ben Carter: +1. We need signals that correlate w/ “would talk to a human about Dedicated/Private” not “someone Googled us”.

Elliot Price: From docs side: we can fire events on doc views w/ URL + referrer + time-on-page bucket + “cta clicked” (e.g., “Contact sales”, “Request VPC”). But we should decide what’s meaningful.

Tyler Benson: Also need to be careful about marketing touch conflict. If we treat every pricing page view as intent, we’ll spam folks who are just comparing.

Camila Reyes: + privacy note: avoid passing full URLs w/ query params into HubSpot notes. We can map to a doc_id / page_category and keep raw in analytics only.

Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Proposed categories:
1) Dedicated intent
2) Private/VPC intent
3) Security/compliance intent
4) Pricing intent
Question is thresholds + “single vs repeated”.

Ben Carter: My strong take: “single page view” is vanity unless it’s a *high-signal page* w/ a downstream action.

Elliot Price: Agree. Docs usage patterns are noisy. Most devs hit docs before they even have an API key.

Nikhil Sharma: So what *is* “high-signal page”?

Markus Klein: From SE land: when they read “VPC deployment” or “KMS/HSM” docs, they’re already in enterprise headspace. Security FAQ also.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: +1 but we should define intent in a way that’s privacy-safe + doesn’t infer “regulated” status from content consumption too aggressively. Better to route as “asked for security artifacts / reviewed security docs” not “healthcare”.

Logan Wright: For scoring we can do: doc_category + engagement level + repetition. But we need a deterministic definition for the event aggregator (daily window).

Nikhil Sharma: Ok. Strawman v1 definitions:
- “Security intent” = (Security FAQ OR SOC2/Compliance docs OR SSO/SAML docs) viewed >= 2 times in 7d OR time-on-page >= 60s (bucketed) AND from authenticated workspace.
- “Private/VPC intent” = any view of Private/VPC deployment docs >= 1 in 14d AND either (a) clicked “Contact sales” CTA OR (b) also viewed “Data residency” / “Retention” doc.
- “Pricing intent” = pricing page view >= 2 in 7d OR clicked “Request quote” (if present) OR visited “Dedicated pricing” section.
- “Dedicated intent” = dedicated docs viewed >= 2 in 14d OR “throughput benchmarks” doc viewed + pricing page view in same 7d.
Thoughts?

Ben Carter: I like the “AND authenticated workspace” piece. Vanity signal is mostly anonymous traffic.

Tyler Benson: Careful: pricing page is on marketing site. We may not always have workspace mapping unless they’re logged in. We *can* map by email if they’re already a contact, but that gets messy.

Camila Reyes: + we shouldn’t start stitching anonymous cookies to product workspaces in v1. Privacy + implementation time.

Elliot Price: For docs, authenticated mapping is easy when they click docs from Console (we have workspace context). For marketing site docs, less so.

Nikhil Sharma: Ok, so v1 rule: only count docs events when we have a workspace_id OR api_key_id context. Marketing pages can be used only when there’s an existing CRM association (known contact) OR we treat it as “soft intent” and don’t auto-task.

Ben Carter: That’s the crux: “soft intent” should show up on the company record but not page the AE.

Markus Klein: +1. If it’s soft intent, it can be an input to scoring, but don’t create a task unless it crosses threshold with product usage signals.

Logan Wright: Implementation-wise, we can output two signal types:
- `intent_low_confidence` (no task; property update only)
- `intent_high_confidence` (eligible for task)
And have suppression windows.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Please ensure the “note text” in HubSpot is category-based, not raw browsing history. Example: “Viewed Security & Compliance documentation (2x, last 7d)” is ok.

Camila Reyes: + add guardrail: if marketing nurture sent in last X days, suppress AE task unless it’s “Private/VPC intent high confidence”. We had overlap issues already.

Tyler Benson: I can own a simple suppression rule in HubSpot workflow: if enrolled in Security Nurture within 5d, don’t create sales task for “security intent only”. (But still log the signal.)

Ben Carter: Back to the definition: for security intent, “2x in 7d” feels fine. But “>=60s” time-on-page is fragile. People leave tabs open.

Elliot Price: We can do better than time-on-page. We can track “scroll depth >= 50%” and “copy code block” / “download PDF” (if any). Those are more intentional.

Nikhil Sharma: Nice. For v1, keep it simple:
- engagement = any of: scroll>=50%, clicked in-page anchor, downloaded artifact, clicked CTA
Then “security intent high confidence” = security docs viewed >= 2 in 14d AND engagement>=1.

Logan Wright: That’s easier for aggregator too: count distinct days with event, and count engaged_views.

Ben Carter: Also want to exclude internal/test orgs + docs team dogfooding. Vanity signal = Redwood employees reading our own docs :)

Elliot Price: Already have internal domain filter on docs analytics. Will share the allow/deny list logic.

Nikhil Sharma: What about pricing intent? It’s the most spam-prone.

Camila Reyes: Pricing page view by itself should be *soft* unless combined with product usage: e.g., spend velocity, repeated 429s, multi-workspace expansion.

Markus Klein: + if they’re already hitting limits (429s) and then look at pricing/dedicated docs, that’s legit.

Ben Carter: So:
- Pricing-only = low confidence
- Pricing + Dedicated docs OR Pricing + usage threshold = high confidence

Nikhil Sharma: Works. Proposed v1 scoring combos:
- `private_intent_high` if (private/vpc docs engaged) OR (private docs viewed >=2 distinct days in 14d)
- `security_intent_high` if (security docs viewed >=2 distinct days in 14d AND engaged_views>=1)
- `dedicated_intent_high` if (dedicated docs viewed >=2 distinct days in 14d) OR (pricing views >=2 in 7d AND dedicated docs >=1 in 14d)
- `pricing_intent_low` if (pricing views >=2 in 7d) without above combos
And we’ll keep `pricing_intent_low` as property-only, no task.

Tyler Benson: + define what counts as “pricing views”. If it’s “/pricing” and “/pricing/dedicated”, ok. Don’t include random blog posts about pricing.

Elliot Price: I’ll give you a `page_category` mapping: `pricing_main`, `pricing_dedicated`, `security_faq`, `docs_private`, `docs_dedicated`, etc.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Please add to ADR: we are not collecting page content, only page_category + counts. Also define retention. (I know there’s a data handling doc coming.)

Logan Wright: For schema v1 we can store `intent_source = docs|marketing_site|console` and restrict routing eligibility to `console` + `docs` with workspace_id.

Ben Carter: Love it. Also: “docs view from Console” is very high signal because they’re already using the product.

Nikhil Sharma: Ok, action items:
- Nikhil: update PRD section “Intent signals v1” w/ the above + clearly call out low vs high confidence.
- Elliot: send page_category mapping + which engagement events are available by Friday.
- Tyler: draft suppression workflow rule for “marketing nurture in last 5d” + “pricing_intent_low no task”.
- Logan: implement aggregator counters: distinct_days_viewed, engaged_views, source.

Camila Reyes: :thumbsup: Also add a note in playbook: if AE sees “pricing_intent_low”, don’t reach out unless there’s another signal.

Markus Klein: +1. Otherwise it’ll feel creepy.

Ben Carter: One more: can we add “security doc views” as a trigger to *offer* the security packet in-app/email rather than a call? softer motion.

Nikhil Sharma: Good idea but probably phase 2. For v1 keep it: route only high-confidence security/private/dedicated intents to tasks; everything else is context.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Works for me, pending I can review the exact HubSpot note copy.

Nikhil Sharma: Will share the template copy in the enablement doc. Thanks all.

Elliot Price: Dropping placeholder link for the mapping doc (WIP): https://docs.redwoodinference.com/internal/page-categories (don’t share externally)

Tyler Benson: Ack. I’ll ping David Kim once we have final property names.

Logan Wright: I’ll align this with ADR v1 fields (`signal_type`, `confidence`, `source`, `window_days`).

Ben Carter: Great. Let’s ship v1 + tune thresholds w/ pilot data rather than perfecting forever.

title: #product: 1745123456
preview:
Alex Chen: Heads-up 1 - our DP private preview funnel is underperforming. 50 sent, 3 completed onboarding. We need quick fixes.
Maria Gomez: Saw that. A few people reported the invite form was too long and asked for billing details up front. That likely kills conversion.
Jordan Blake: Also the onboarding checklist references an old role name for service accounts. Confusing and blocks step 3.
Samir Patel: And corporate mail filters are dropping the slack invite link for some domains. Ill add an email fallback with a one-click token.
Alex Chen: Can we split into two outreach paths: fast try (very light touch) vs design partner (commitment asks)?
Maria Gomez: Yes. Fast path = no org questions, single CTA, basic setup doc. DP path = commitment form, NDA, 4-week feedback cadence.
Jordan Blake: Suggest for DP ask we specify per-feature asks instead of vague "feedback": e.g., quality eval on 2 prompts, latency tolerance test, repro steps for any failures.
Samir Patel: Will prototype email fallback and short instructions doc. ETA today EOD. Test cohort id `dp-fast-2025-06-02`.
Iris (eng-bot): deploy: onboarding-docs v2.2 promoted to staging at 2025-06-02T08:12Z
Maria Gomez: PR fixes: `docs/onboard#153` (role names) and `forms/invite#21` (remove org-size mandatory).
Jordan Blake: I'll write an A/B plan for invite copy: baseline vs shorter CTA + incentive (credits + early-access flag). Small test: 100 recipients each.
Alex Chen: Good. Primary KPI = week-1 activation (complete onboarding). Secondary: feedback session attendance, qualitative signal. Goal: lift activation from ~6% -> 25%.
Maria Gomez: New DP acceptance criteria draft:
- attend 4 feedback sessions over 30 days
- provide 3 production-representative prompts/flows
- sign NDA + accept data handling checklist
Jordan Blake: Make feedback sessions 20-30 mins and scoped per feature. Add template questions to make sessions actionable.
Samir Patel: I'll run the quick A/B test with email fallback and track conversions. Will post results in this thread.
Alex Chen: Schedule a 30m sync at 14:00 PST today to triage blockers and decide on rollout. Bring test framework and acceptance copy.
Iris (eng-bot): calendar: DP triage 14:00 PST today 1 attendees: Alex, Maria, Jordan, Samir
Maria Gomez: I'll also add a small incentive line to invite copy: "$500 cloud credits + early feature access" as an experiment.
Jordan Blake: Will prepare updated invite copy and a short list of interview templates.
Samir Patel: Logging cohort: `dp-fast-2025-06-02` in tracker. Will share raw click/open data before sync.
Alex Chen: Action items: Samir (fallback), Maria (docs + invite copy), Jordan (A/B plan + templates). Quick wins first.
Maria Gomez: Adding success metrics to tracker: activation %, onboarding completion rate, feedback compliance (% sessions attended), and week-2 NPS.
Jordan Blake: If conversion improves, we can expand to private preview clients next week.
Alex Chen: Thanks team. Ping me with any blockers prior to the 14:00 sync.
Maria Gomez: :thumbsup:
Samir Patel: on it.
Jordan Blake: :eyes:

title: #product: 1745004321
preview:
Priya Patel: Quick sync — thinking about a smaller, cleaner ratecard. Goal: fewer SKUs, clearer add-on semantics. Thoughts?
Liam Chen: Agree on fewer SKUs. Concern: we break negotiated enterprise commits if mapping isn't explicit.
Samira Ortiz: We should define a deterministic mapping from old tiers -> new tiers + conversion credits. Minimal surprise for billing.
Ethan Park: +1. Also need launch criteria: (1) mapping doc approved by finance, (2) migration tool to translate historical usage, (3) fallback plan for top 10 customers by ARR.
Priya Patel: Suggested rollout phases: alpha (internal + 3 low-risk customers), beta (wider opt-in), GA (auto-migrate with opt-out window).
Liam Chen: For opt-out window, propose 90 days. Need legal signoff for defaults.
Samira Ortiz: Product ask: add-on semantics must be per-feature (e.g., streaming, embeddings) not per-token-only. Some customers buy predictable latency lanes.
Ethan Park: Ops note: Dedicated customers expect capacity parity — any SKU change must preserve reserved capacity mapping.
Priya Patel: Action items: Samira to draft mapping table + FAQ, Liam to run impact query on top 50 accounts, Ethan to draft ops migration runbook.
Samira Ortiz: Draft matrix started. Preview: ```old_tier -> new_tier, conversion_rate, preserves_reserved_capacity``` e.g. `pro-reserved -> performance-pro, 1:1, yes`
product-bot: Created tracking ticket: PROD-8421 — ratecard-simplify migration epic (link: https://redwood.example/t/PROD-8421)
Liam Chen: Quick question: do we introduce a new per-feature add-on SKU for "priority routing"? That was a common ask.
Priya Patel: I prefer a single "QoS add-on" SKU with parameters (requests/min, priority weight). Easier to document and sell.
Samira Ortiz: Agree. Sales-friendly naming: "Priority Lane" + short table of guarantees. Keep price bands simple.
Ethan Park: Launch gating: validated billing for 5 pilot customers + no more than 2 post-migration billing anomalies.
Priya Patel: Timeline proposal: planning this sprint, mapping + legal next, pilots in 6 weeks, GA in 12. If blockers, push GA back.
Liam Chen: I'll share the top-50 impact numbers by EOD Wednesday.
Samira Ortiz: I'll put the mapping + FAQ in the product drive and ping sales for feedback.
Priya Patel: Thanks — we'll reconvene after the mapping draft. :thumbsup:

Liam Chen: Agree on fewer SKUs. Concern: we break negotiated enterprise commits if mapping isn't explicit.
Samira Ortiz: We should define a deterministic mapping from old tiers -> new tiers + conversion credits. Minimal surprise for billing.
Ethan Park: +1. Also need launch criteria: (1) mapping doc approved by finance, (2) migration tool to translate historical usage, (3) fallback plan for top 10 customers by ARR.
Priya Patel: Suggested rollout phases: alpha (internal + 3 low-risk customers), beta (wider opt-in), GA (auto-migrate with opt-out window).
Liam Chen: For opt-out window, propose 90 days. Need legal signoff for defaults.
Samira Ortiz: Product ask: add-on semantics must be per-feature (e.g., streaming, embeddings) not per-token-only. Some customers buy predictable latency lanes.
Ethan Park: Ops note: Dedicated customers expect capacity parity — any SKU change must preserve reserved capacity mapping.
Priya Patel: Action items: Samira to draft mapping table + FAQ, Liam to run impact query on top 50 accounts, Ethan to draft ops migration runbook.
Samira Ortiz: Draft matrix started. Preview: ```old_tier -> new_tier, conversion_rate, preserves_reserved_capacity``` e.g. `pro-reserved -> performance-pro, 1:1, yes`

title: #product: 1744444444
preview:
Ava Chen: kicking off intake for a scoped PRD — 'express path' for high-priority enterprise tenants. problem: some customers need sub-100ms token latency for critical flows and are willing to trade budget for predictable SLOs. want to capture goals, non-goals, constraints, and launch metrics here.
Marcus Li: quick q — is this a dedicated-only feature or available on hosted?
Ava Chen: dedicated-first. we can discuss hosted later as a stretch. non-goal: no changes to public API surface.
Priya Singh: high-level goals: 1) guarantee p50 < 80ms p95 < 150ms for premium tenants under normal load, 2) provide cost-cap knob per-tenant, 3) rollout controls (canary + instant rollback).
Diego Alvarez: infra constraints: we'll need reserved GPU slots + prewarmed KV cache. current Dedicated pool for GPUs can reserve up to 20% per-customer without infra changes. beyond that requires procurement lead time.
Ava Chen: success metrics proposal (draft):
```
- latency: p50 < 80ms, p95 < 150ms (measured at routing edge)
- availability: 99.9% (monthly)
- cost delta: < 2x baseline per-token for configured tenants
- rollout failure rate: < 1% per-week post rollout
```
Marcus Li: acceptance criteria ideas: 1) automated smoke harness that runs synthetic flows at load; 2) perf regression test against pinned model; 3) alerting if cache hit rate drops below 60%.
Priya Singh: also need clear non-goals: no tenant-specific model training, no new auth surface, no persistent customer feature flags beyond routing config.
Diego Alvarez: edge cases we should document: 1) burst traffic > 10x baseline -> fallback policy to best-effort pool, 2) model eviction due to quantization mismatch, 3) network partition between control plane and private VPC.
Ava Chen: customer ask summary: 1) low-latency lane they can opt into, 2) budget hard cap, 3) instant rollback API. They indicated willingness to sign 12mo reserved capacity for latency guarantees.
Marcus Li: timeline ask? product suggests alpha in 8 weeks, beta 14 weeks. engineering realistic?
Diego Alvarez: alpha for a single tenant (proof-of-concept) in ~6 weeks if we limit to 1 GPU type and reuse current prewarm infra. broad alpha depends on procurement.
Priya Singh: risk register (short): - cost blowout if cache miss high, - false confidence from synthetic tests, - customer upgrade churn at rollback.
Ava Chen: next steps: 1) Marcus to draft PRD one-pager with proposed API knobs + rollout UX by EOD Tue, 2) Diego to run capacity check and list infra gaps by Fri, 3) Priya to draft acceptance test scenarios and SLO dashboard fields. tagging @Marcus Li @Diego Alvarez @Priya Singh.
ops-bot: Deployment note: created project ticket INF-6723 for capacity investigation (link: https://redwood.internal/INF-6723).

title: #product: 1743620901
preview:
Nikhil Sharma: Need final pass on customer-facing messaging for the overload canary. Two things: (1) API error body copy + code naming, (2) status page wording if we see elevated 503/429 during rollout. Want to ship canary in us-east tomorrow AM.

Henry Cho: +1. Goal is “clear + actionable” without scaring folks. Also want consistency between docs, support macros, and what status page says.

Christine Wong: From eng side: canary is *only* enabling the new early-shed paths + consistent Retry-After. We *will* return more explicit overload responses instead of timeouts. So customers may notice 503s where previously they saw hangs.

Wei Chen: Can you paste the proposed error payload copy? I need to update docs snippet + examples.

Nikhil Sharma: Draft payload (additive fields):
```
HTTP/1.1 503 Service Unavailable
Retry-After: 2
x-redwood-correlation-id: rcid_01HT...

{
  "error": {
    "type": "overload_error",
    "code": "hosted_overloaded",
    "message": "The service is temporarily overloaded. Please retry with exponential backoff.",
    "retry_after_seconds": 2,
    "correlation_id": "rcid_01HT..."
  }
}
```
Open questions: code string (`hosted_overloaded` vs `capacity_exhausted`), and status page copy (“overloaded” vs “capacity constrained”).

Dev Patel: Support preference: “overloaded” is immediately understandable. “capacity constrained” triggers enterprise escalations / SLA questions. But we need to be explicit that retrying aggressively makes it worse.

Owen Phillips: On incidents, we also want to avoid people thinking it’s a global outage. If it’s only a subset (region/model), status page needs scoped language + link to retry guidance.

Lauren Bishop: For Dedicated customers, we should avoid implying this affects their reserved capacity (unless it does). Status page should say “Hosted API” and ideally region-limited.

Ethan Park: Re HTTP mapping: gateway will use 429 when it’s per-tenant/per-route rate/concurrency policy (client is above agreed limit), 503 when it’s platform/runtime saturation or dependency brownout. That should be reflected in message text.

Ibrahim Khan: SDKs can automatically parse `Retry-After` and suggest backoff. But the message string should be generic and not imply “your fault” for 503.

Henry Cho: Agree. For 429, we can say “rate limit exceeded” + “slow down.” For 503, “temporarily overloaded” + “retry after X.”

Mina Haddad: On fallback: if customer configured fallback model, we’ll attempt it only when breaker open and within guardrails. We shouldn’t mention fallback in the generic error message because it won’t always happen.

Nikhil Sharma: What about `code` naming? `hosted_overloaded` feels a bit… blunt. Alternative from earlier doc was `overload_shed`.

Christine Wong: Internally we have reasons like `queue_depth_high`, `tokens_per_sec_saturated`, etc. But customer-facing should be stable + not too many. My vote: `overloaded` as the code, but namespaced. e.g. `overload` + `overload_shed` + `overload_circuit_open`?

Ethan Park: We already aligned in ADR that `code` should be stable and not expose too much infra detail. I’d keep it to:
- `rate_limited` (429)
- `overloaded` (503)
Optionally add `reason` for debugging, but we said we might omit initially.

Wei Chen: For docs, fewer codes is better. But we do need to answer: “should I retry?” If code is `overloaded`, then yes (with backoff + respect Retry-After). If `rate_limited`, yes but slow down / adjust concurrency.

Dev Patel: Please keep the *message* aligned with our support macro language. Suggested strings:
- 429 message: “Request rate limit exceeded for your account. Reduce request rate and retry.”
- 503 message: “Service temporarily overloaded. Retry after the specified delay.”
No mention of “exponential backoff” in the literal message? Some customers copy/paste it into runbooks; others mis-implement it.

Ibrahim Khan: I’d prefer we *do* mention backoff in docs, but message can be simpler. SDK examples will show exponential backoff + jitter.

Nikhil Sharma: Ok. Proposed final:
- `type`: `overload_error` (for both?) or separate types?

Ethan Park: Separate `type` is useful.
- 429: `type: "rate_limit_error"`, `code: "rate_limited"`
- 503: `type: "overload_error"`, `code: "overloaded"`
Both include `correlation_id` and `retry_after_seconds` when present.

Henry Cho: +1. Also make sure status page doesn’t say “rate limiting” when it’s overload. Different customer reaction.

Owen Phillips: Status page wording suggestion (when canary causes noticeable change):
“Investigating: Some customers may see elevated 503 responses on the Hosted API in us-east. Requests are failing fast with Retry-After guidance rather than timing out. Please retry with backoff and contact support with correlation IDs if persistent.”

Lauren Bishop: That’s still a little technical. Can we remove “failing fast” and keep “may see elevated 503s” + “retry after delay”?

Elliot Price: Docs voice is usually: “You may temporarily receive `503` with code `overloaded` during periods of high demand. Respect `Retry-After` and use exponential backoff with jitter.” For status page, shorter.

Nikhil Sharma: Ok, status page drafts:
A) “We’re investigating increased error rates (503) for a subset of Hosted API requests in us-east. Please retry requests and respect Retry-After.”
B) “We’re investigating elevated 503s for some Hosted API traffic in us-east. Requests may be rejected during high demand to protect overall latency.”

Dev Patel: Prefer A. B invites “why are you rejecting me” emails.

Mina Haddad: +1 A. Also, can we add “subset of models” if applicable? We might only see it on chat routes.

Christine Wong: During canary we expect most impact on chat streaming + long prompts (prefill heavy). I’d rather not mention prompt lengths publicly. But we can scope to “Chat Completions in us-east” if that’s accurate.

Ethan Park: We can template it with fields we know from dashboards: region + route family. Oncall can fill in “chat” vs “embeddings.”

Owen Phillips: For incident comms, we also want a resolved message template: “Mitigated by adjusting overload thresholds / rolling back canary. Continuing to monitor.”

Henry Cho: One more: do we say “overloaded” or “high demand”? Some companies avoid “overloaded.”

Nikhil Sharma: My take: status page can say “high demand” but error code says `overloaded`. That’s fine.

Wei Chen: Works for docs: “code: overloaded” is explicit. For status page: “high demand causing elevated 503s.”

Ibrahim Khan: Confirming header behavior: always set `Retry-After` on 503 overload and 429 rate limit? Some proxies strip it.

Ethan Park: That’s the intent. If `Retry-After` missing, SDK falls back to default (e.g., 1–2s + jitter). But we should avoid that. Christine, any known routes missing it?

Christine Wong: There was one edge path on non-streaming chat in staging. Fixed in latest build; will re-verify before canary.

Nikhil Sharma: Final proposal (customer-facing):
- Error code: `overloaded` (503), `rate_limited` (429)
- Message strings:
  - 503: “Service temporarily overloaded. Retry after the specified delay.”
  - 429: “Request rate limit exceeded. Reduce request rate and retry.”
- Status page template (Investigating): “We’re investigating increased 503 responses for a subset of Hosted API requests in <region>. Please retry and respect Retry-After.”
Everyone ok?

Lauren Bishop: Approved. Please ensure we say “Hosted API” explicitly.

Dev Patel: Approved. I’ll update support macro + escalation checklist to ask for correlation_id + region + model.

Elliot Price: Approved. I’ll align docs language to match these exact strings and keep the backoff details in the guidance section.

Mina Haddad: Approved. Routing oncall note: if we roll out fallback in future, we’ll message separately.

Henry Cho: :thumbsup: Ship it. Please drop the final strings in the PRD and link the status page template for oncall.

Nikhil Sharma: Will do. @Wei Chen @Elliot Price I’ll paste the final JSON examples in the docs ticket as well.

Wei Chen: :thumbsup: send over when ready.

Owen Phillips: I’ll add the status page templates to the oncall runbook + comms doc. Also reminder: if canary causes measurable customer impact, we should proactively post even if it’s “subset.”

Christine Wong: Cool. I’ll confirm `Retry-After` + correlation id headers in canary build and report back before 9am deploy.

title: #product: 1743300123
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads-up 1: we need to finalize the mini-wave for post-launch next sprint. Customer impact signals suggest prioritizing reliability and a few rapid UX wins. Proposal: 70% bugs, 30% enhancements. Anyone opposed?

Ben Cho: 70/30 seems reasonable given the telemetry spikes. Curious what the bug shortlist looks like though.

Nina Patel: Agree on the split. Also request we include one experiment in enhancements that we can measure in 2 weeks (not more). Sam can own the metric.

Sam Ortiz (PMM): I can own the experiment metric. Suggest we A/B test the onboarding CTA (two copy variants) and measure 7-day activation. Low risk and fast.

Priya K (Eng): Capacity check: 4 devs available Tue-Thu. If we do 70/30, I'd allocate 3 on bugs and 1 on enhancements/experiment. Need QA coverage too.

Ava Chen: Good. Constraints:
- Freeze database schema migrations for this wave.
- No new heavy infra work (keep deploy surface small).
- All tickets must include an "impact_statement" and a short rollback plan.

Ben Cho: Draft bug list (short):
```
- BUG-5120: prompt truncation for >64k inputs (P0) 1 customer-reported
- BUG-5175: /embeddings latency regressions under 95th pct (P0)
- BUG-5201: auth token rotation race condition (P1)
- BUG-5233: dashboard refresh shows stale metrics for 30s (P1)
- BUG-5300: autoscaler mislabels idle pods leading to scale-down (P2)
```
I'll add impact_statement + acceptance tests by EOD.

Nina Patel: For the UX experiment: ENH-3301 onboarding CTA copy variants + tracking. Sam will define the KPI and sample size. Keep the experiment to 5% traffic.

Sam Ortiz (PMM): I'll push the KPI: 7d activation increase, 95% CI, min N=3k. If we can't meet sample in 2 weeks, we roll experiment back.

Priya K (Eng): Patch branch: patch/miniwave-3301. I'll create PRs for each P0 first. CI will run nightly. QA-bot: can you run targeted regressions for embeddings and auth?

QA-bot: regression-suite queued for patch/miniwave-3301 -> https://ci.redwood.ai/runs/miniwave-3301 (logs attached). Initial failures: BUG-5175 latency spike reprobed, attaching perf trace.

Ben Cho: Acked. Priya, can you take BUG-5175 first, then BUG-5120?

Priya K (Eng): Yes, starting on 5175. ETA 1.5 days for root cause + patch. If root cause is infra-related, may need ops assist.

Ava Chen: Decision checklist before we ship canary:
- Finalized ticket list & owners (Ben)
- impact_statement + acceptance tests in each ticket (owners)
- patch branch + preview build (Priya)
- experiment gating (Sam) + analytics dashboard (Nina)
- rollback window scheduled (Thu 10:00-12:00 UTC)

Ben Cho: I'll post updated ticket list and owners by 3pm.

Sam Ortiz (PMM): I'll set up the dashboard and the experiment flag in prod with 5% gating.

Nina Patel: Quick ask 1 can we keep a hotfix pager rotation for the first 48h after canary? Last time we needed fast patching.

Ava Chen: Yes, create a 48h hotfix rota. Ben, please coordinate with on-call.

Priya K (Eng): Created patch + PRs. Link: https://git.redwood.ai/r/redwood/miniwave-3301. Reviewer assigned: Dave (if available).

Ben Cho: No objections. Locking the 70/30 split and the plan above. Will follow up with stakeholder summary. :white_check_mark:

Sam Ortiz (PMM): Thanks team. I'll monitor the experiment funnel and report daily.

Ava Chen: Great. If anything urgent pops up, ping here or DM.

```
- BUG-5175: /embeddings latency regressions under 95th pct (P0)
- BUG-5201: auth token rotation race condition (P1)
- BUG-5233: dashboard refresh shows stale metrics for 30s (P1)
- BUG-5300: autoscaler mislabels idle pods leading to scale-down (P2)
```
I'll add impact_statement + acceptance tests by EOD.

Nina Patel: For the UX experiment: ENH-3301 onboarding CTA copy variants + tracking. Sam will define the KPI and sample size. Keep the experiment to 5% traffic.

Sam Ortiz (PMM): I'll push the KPI: 7d activation increase, 95% CI, min N=3k. If we can't meet sample in 2 weeks, we roll experiment back.

Priya K (Eng): Patch branch: patch/miniwave-3301. I'll create PRs for each P0 first. CI will run nightly. QA-bot: can you run targeted regressions for embeddings and auth?

QA-bot: regression-suite queued for patch/miniwave-3301 -> https://ci.redwood.ai/runs/miniwave-3301 (logs attached). Initial failures: BUG-5175 latency spike reprobed, attaching perf trace.

Ben Cho: Acked. Priya, can you take BUG-5175 first, then BUG-5120?

Priya K (Eng): Yes, starting on 5175. ETA 1.5 days for root cause + patch. If root cause is infra-related, may need ops assist.

Ava Chen: Decision checklist before we ship canary:
- Finalized ticket list & owners (Ben)
- impact_statement + acceptance tests in each ticket (owners)
- patch branch + preview build (Priya)
- experiment gating (Sam) + analytics dashboard (Nina)
- rollback window scheduled (Thu 10:00-12:00 UTC)

Ben Cho: I'll post updated ticket list and owners by 3pm.

Sam Ortiz (PMM): I'll set up the dashboard and the experiment flag in prod with 5% gating.

Nina Patel: Quick ask 1 can we keep a hotfix pager rotation for the first 48h after canary? Last time we needed fast patching.

Ava Chen: Yes, create a 48h hotfix rota. Ben, please coordinate with on-call.

Priya K (Eng): Created patch + PRs. Link: https://git.redwood.ai/r/redwood/miniwave-3301. Reviewer assigned: Dave (if available).

Ben Cho: No objections. Locking the 70/30 split and the plan above. Will follow up with stakeholder summary. :white_check_mark:

Sam Ortiz (PMM): Thanks team. I'll monitor the experiment funnel and report daily.

Ava Chen: Great. If anything urgent pops up, ping here or DM.

title: #product: 1743216543
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick sync — we need to lock the follow-on mini-wave for post-launch fixes. Proposal: 60% bugs (customer-facing/regressions), 40% enhancements (quick UX/metric wins). Can we confirm owners + any blockers by EOD? cc @Ben Cho @Cara Singh @Jess Q :calendar:

Ben Cho: 60/40 sounds fine. I'll triage and post a prioritized bug list by 5pm. I think top 6 are unblockers for customers. :thumbsup:

Cara Singh: +1. Suggest we only commit P0/P1 and one P2 that unblocks onboarding. Also recommend a 1-day hotfix lane for any regressions that show up in QA.

Jess Q (QA): We need acceptance criteria for each bug. If devs can add a short test case in the ticket, QA automation can run nightly against the patch branch. Otherwise manual time will blow up.

Ava Chen: Good call Jess. Ben, please add an "acceptance_tests" bullet in each ticket. Also: freeze API schema changes for this wave — no breaking changes without an explicit post-launch rollout plan.

Ben Cho: Posted list (brief):
```
- BUG-4712: embeddings ingestion failure for long inputs (P0) — affects 3 customers
- BUG-4831: token billing mismatch in /usage endpoint (P0)
- BUG-4902: streaming reconnect drops final chunk (P1)
- BUG-4920: console graph mislabels latency bucket >1s (P1)
- BUG-4999: dedicated pool autoscale thrash under burst (P2)
- BUG-5010: minor UX copy in onboarding modal (P2)
```
I'll add acceptance_tests to each ticket by EOD.

Cara Singh: Dev capacity note — we have 3 devs available Wed-Fri (excluding on-call). Recommend 2 devs on P0/P1 fixes, 1 dev on the enhancement bucket. If a regression hits, we pull the enhancement dev into hotfix lane.

Dave R (Eng): We can spin up a patch branch: patch/miniwave-1743. Can allocate 2 ICs (Ben, Priya) + 1 reviewer (me). If test coverage added, CI time will increase but manageable. On-call can be looped for canary.

deploy-bot: deploy-preview created for patch/miniwave-1743 -> https://staging.redwood.ai/preview/miniwave-1743 (auto-tests queued)

Jess Q (QA): Running smoke suite now against the preview. Two failures so far (streaming reconnect + billing mismatch) — will attach logs to BUG-4902 and BUG-4831.

Ben Cho: Acknowledged, will ping Priya to start on streaming reconnect first.

Ava Chen: Decision checklist:
- Lock list by 5pm today (Ben)
- Owners assigned (Ben/Dave)
- Acceptance tests in tickets (Ben/Dev)
- Patch branch + preview (Dave)
- Canary on Thursday morning to 5% (deploy-bot)

Cara Singh: Tiny ask — can we reserve one post-canary rollback window in case of perf regression? 2 hours.

Dave R (Eng): Yes — schedule rollback window Thu 11:00-13:00 UTC. I'll be on standby.

Ava Chen: Ok locking plan. If anyone has strong objections or must-add items, speak now. Otherwise we'll proceed with the 60/40 split and the runbook above. :white_check_mark:

Ben Cho: No objections here. I'll post the final ticket list + owners in this thread by 4:30p.

Jess Q (QA): Will post test matrix and assign QA owners by EOD as well.

Cara Singh: :thumbsup: thanks all.

Ava Chen: Thanks — will follow up with a short summary for stakeholders after EOD.

title: #product: 1741596602
preview:
Marissa Cole: need quick alignment on pricing/packaging + messaging for Quantization Profile Library before next beta update. Q: is this “included in Optimize” (paid) or available to all (opt-in) and Optimize is just the tooling/evals? also wording: “library” vs “recommendation”.

Paula Mendes: +1. telemetry side we can expose impact panels (cost/token deltas, fallback_rate, etc) either way, but packaging impacts what we promise. If we say Optimize includes it, ppl will expect “turn it on and savings happen” + recommendations.

Miguel Santos: Sales POV: if it’s included in Optimize, it’s a clean upsell story (“use Optimize to cut costs”). but enterprise will ask: is the quantization itself gated, or just dashboards/support? we should avoid surprise paywall during beta.

Markus Klein: design partner feedback from Acme AI + Northstar: they like that it’s explicit per-route, but they’re already asking “which profile should I pick?” If we don’t have recs in v1, calling it “recommendations” is going to backfire.

Alex Martinez: DevEx: for docs, “library” reads like catalog / curated set. “recommendation” implies auto-selected, or at least guided by workload. right now we only have “choose a profile ref” + compatibility warnings + eval playbook.

Elliot Price: wording nit: “Quantization Profile Library (opt-in)” is fine. we can add a line: “Redwood does not enable profiles by default; you choose a profile per route.” If marketing wants “recommendation” we can scope it as “recommended alias” only, but that’s still risky.

Dylan Brooks: Console UX can show “Suggested: (coming soon)” but we shouldn’t ship UI that says recommended unless policy engine actually returns one. current flow: user picks from allowed profiles list, sees compatibility + expected deltas (if available) + confirm modal.

Marissa Cole: ok so direction: feature name stays “Library”. Optimize packaging: is it fair to say Library access is available on all paid tiers (or all customers) but “Optimize” includes advanced analytics/retention + guidance? I’m trying to avoid gating the runtime capability behind Optimize if we’re saying it’s part of platform serving.

Miguel Santos: +1 to not gating runtime. A bunch of customers see quantization as table-stakes infra capability. We can monetize: longer retention, dashboards, eval automation integration, premium support / guardrail SLAs.

Paula Mendes: from rollout/telemetry PM: we should be careful not to promise quality guarantees as part of base plan. The guardrails are there, but customers still own decision to enable + evaluate. Optimize tier could include “evaluation pack” / “regression monitoring”.

Markus Klein: agree. also, for beta, we’re already doing hands-on with partners. If we tie it to Optimize now, procurement gets messy.

Alex Martinez: for docs + SDK examples I’ll avoid “recommended” entirely. we can describe “baseline (default)” vs “profile-enabled”. maybe add “profile selection is customer-driven; Redwood may publish new profiles over time.”

Elliot Price: + we should add a FAQ entry: “Is this part of Optimize?” Answer: “Profiles are available as an opt-in feature; Optimize adds tooling to measure and manage cost/quality tradeoffs.” keep it vague enough until pricing is final.

Marissa Cole: what about the term “library” vs “catalog”? library sounds like curated artifacts w/ versions, deprecation, etc (which is true). catalog overlaps with model catalog.

Dylan Brooks: +1 keep library. in Console, we can label section “Quantization profiles” and the picker reads “Choose a profile (opt-in)”. no need to show “Library” everywhere.

Paula Mendes: also re: “recommended alias”: platform thread was worried about silent changes. if we ever offer “recommended/latest”, that’s a separate capability and should be explicit (and likely Optimize add-on). not for v1.

Miguel Santos: :thumbsup: for sales deck, I’ll message: “Quantization Profile Library (opt-in) + Route-level controls + Observability + Fast rollback”. then “Optimize add-on: evaluation + monitoring workflows.”

Ava Chen: catching up—strongly prefer we do NOT brand this as “recommendations” until we can stand behind it (auto-selection, clear policy, and change control). “Library” is accurate and safer. Packaging: default is platform capability; Optimize can bundle advanced measurement + guidance.

Marissa Cole: great. action items:
- Marissa: update launch brief/pricing notes: Library is platform feature; Optimize positions around measurement + guidance (no “recommendations” wording).
- Elliot/Alex: align docs copy + FAQ.
- Miguel/Markus: update beta/customer messaging.
- Dylan: ensure Console strings avoid “recommended” and confirm modals emphasize opt-in + rollback.

Elliot Price: ack. I’ll draft FAQ + terminology section today and send for review.

Alex Martinez: I’ll update docs PR copy to remove “recommendation” mentions and add a sentence about profile cadence + version pinning.

Miguel Santos: works. will sync w/ CS so they don’t say “Optimize feature” on calls.

Paula Mendes: I’ll add a note to telemetry spec: what’s baseline vs Optimize-tier dashboards, so we don’t end up gating core safety metrics.

Marissa Cole: thanks all. closing the loop after leadership pricing review next week.

title: #product: 1741104321
preview:
Priya Rao: Quick proposal: add ephemeral/session-scoped saved queries to Console analytics. Server-side TTL (default 24h), sharable with short-lived tokens. Use case: temporary filter experiments without cluttering org library.
Ben Carter: Agreed. API change: add scope enum (session|route|org) and ttl_seconds. Include a convert_to_org endpoint.
Asha Kumar: UX: show an "ephemeral" pill with relative countdown (e.g., "23h left"). Hover shows exact expiry. Sort persistent saves above ephemeral ones.
Priya Rao: Sharing: signed link tokens that expire. If token used after expiry return 410.
Ben Carter: Telemetry: emit events with fields saved_query.id, scope, created_by, expires_at, attached_routes. When executed include model and token_count_estimate. Executions billed against route/model quotas.
Asha Kumar: Guardrails: block attaching session-scoped queries to alerts/exports; require conversion to org scope.
ops-bot: Note: consider client-side cost estimate before running heavy ad-hoc queries.
Priya Rao: Backend sketch: store ephemeral metadata in Redis with TTL and a lightweight DB row for audit. Conversion copies into persistent table. Reconcile job for missed expirations.
Ben Carter: Limits: cap active ephemeral saves per user (30). Ephemerals do not count against org library quotas. Executions still count toward API/model quotas.
Asha Kumar: Example API: POST /v1/saved_queries { name: "temp-latency-filter", scope: "session", ttl_seconds: 86400, filters: [{route: "/v1/chat", model: "redwood-small", latency_ms: "<200"}], shareable: true }
Priya Rao: Owners: Ben (backend), Priya (product), Asha (UX). ETA: RFC next week, staging in ~3 sprints.
Ben Carter: I'll add telemetry + quota changes to backend design doc.
Asha Kumar: I'll draft UI mocks for list, flyout, and convert flow.
Priya Rao: Filing RFC now. :+1:

title: #product: 1740779002
preview:
Nikhil Sharma: We need to lock a definition for “time-to-first-success” (TTFS) for Quickstart Templates v1. Otherwise launch KPI dashboard is going to be bikeshedding forever. What’s the *first success*?

Henry Cho: +1. Also needs to be something we can measure reliably from telemetry (not “user felt happy”).

Talia Benson: Can we list candidate definitions + what events we can actually emit? Then pick one for v1 + a stretch metric.

Monica Patel: From DevEx POV “success” should map to the user seeing something *work* locally: they scaffold template, set env vars, run, get a response. If we make it “deploy to prod” we’ll miss most PLG users.

Elliot Price: Agree. Docs framing is “get to first working request” then “deploy + observe” as next steps. TTFS should align with the first milestone.

Wei Chen: Users in interviews basically said “if I can ask one question and it returns an answer, I’m unblocked.” They don’t think in terms of traces/metrics until later.

Logan Wright: Instrumentation reality check: We can tag template-originated traffic (header + SDK metadata) and log backend events for request success/failure. CLI can emit client-side events too, but those don’t always make it (ad blockers, offline, corporate restrictions). Server-side signal is most robust.

Nikhil Sharma: Candidate A: TTFS = time from first template init -> first successful LLM response (HTTP 200) with template tag.

Henry Cho: What about people who don’t use CLI (GitHub template) and copy/paste? If the template tag only shows up with our instrumentation, we might undercount.

Monica Patel: The tag should be in the template code path regardless of CLI. CLI is optional. For GitHub template, it’s still our template—same middleware adds header / request metadata.

Logan Wright: Yeah: template code can include `X-Redwood-Template: rag-agent@<version>` (or similar) and SDK adds `client.user_agent` / metadata. But note: proxy issues with unknown headers came up in another thread. We may need it opt-in or use query param? (also gross)

Nikhil Sharma: Right, but for KPI we can fallback to “SDK app_name field” or a low-risk header allowlist. We already have the gateway whitelist PR queued.

Priya Shah: From support side, I’d like TTFS to reflect when a user reaches a point where they would *not* file a ticket. That’s closer to “first request works” than “template cloned.” Most tickets are around auth/401, rate limits/429, streaming disconnects.

Henry Cho: So success shouldn’t be “first request attempted”. It should be “first request succeeded”. But what counts as succeeded? For chat streaming, you could get partial tokens then disconnect.

Monica Patel: For streaming, we can define success as “received at least one content token and stream ended cleanly” (finish_reason not error). But that’s harder to measure server-side.

Logan Wright: Server side we can measure: request completed with 2xx + `completion_tokens > 0` + no `stream_error` flag. We can also log a structured event when stream terminates.

Alex Martinez: Let’s keep v1 simple and robust. TTFS should be “first 2xx response from a template-tagged request” for either non-streaming or streaming. Then add a secondary “first clean streaming completion” later.

Nikhil Sharma: Ok but do we require retrieval to run? It’s “RAG + agent” template. If user runs the basic chat path without indexing anything, is that success?

Wei Chen: Template walkthrough starts with “run with sample docs” + query. But we also have a minimal “ask the assistant” endpoint for sanity check.

Elliot Price: Docs will have Step 1: “verify connectivity” (simple prompt) and Step 2: “RAG query over sample docs.” If we use Step 2 as success, TTFS will look worse.

Henry Cho: I’m worried product leadership will interpret TTFS as “time to RAG working” not “time to API works.” But we can report both.

Talia Benson: Proposal: define TTFS_v1 as *Connectivity Success*; define TTFS_RAG as *RAG Success* (secondary). Both start at the same “start clock.”

Nikhil Sharma: What’s the start clock? Signup? API key created? template init?

Logan Wright: We can do multiple start points. But cleanest PLG funnel is: `signup_completed` (backend), `api_key_created` (backend), then template events. The first template event we control reliably is “first template-tagged request seen.” But that makes TTFS always ~0.

Monica Patel: For DevEx funnel: start = when user runs `redwood-templates init` OR when app starts (first run). CLI can emit `template_init` and app can emit `template_app_start`.

Henry Cho: If CLI event is missing, we can fall back to `first_template_request_ts` and treat TTFS as undefined/missing for that user. But KPI dashboard will be messy.

Nikhil Sharma: What if start = first time we see `X-Redwood-Template` header *ever* for that user, and success = first 2xx for that same tag? Then TTFS measures “time from first attempt to first success” (still meaningful).

Priya Shah: That aligns with support: how long between “they tried” and “it worked.”

Alex Martinez: +1. That’s measurable and doesn’t depend on the CLI making it through corporate network.

Elliot Price: Works with GitHub template too. Start clock becomes “first template-tagged request (any status)”.

Henry Cho: Edge case: user’s first template request is a health check or embeddings call. Does that count?

Monica Patel: We can scope success event types. For RAG+agent template, success should be a chat/generate call from the orchestrator endpoint (not just embeddings).

Logan Wright: We can include an attribute like `template.flow = connectivity|rag|agent` and `template.endpoint = /chat` etc. But be careful about cardinality.

Nikhil Sharma: Can we hardcode a small enum? ex:
- `template_flow=connectivity` (simple “hello world” prompt)
- `template_flow=rag_query` (retrieval executed)
- `template_flow=agent_toolcall` (tool call executed)

Monica Patel: Yes. We can emit that as a low-cardinality tag on requests + in logs.

Henry Cho: How do we detect “retrieval executed” and “tool call executed”? That’s in-app logic, not gateway.

Monica Patel: Template code can set the tag when it enters those code paths. Same as we’re doing for observability spans.

Logan Wright: If it’s a request header, some proxies may block it. Alternative is to attach it as SDK metadata (body field) but that’s risky / privacy. Better: keep request header minimal (template name+version) and flow tags as internal spans/metrics.

Nikhil Sharma: Let’s not put flow tags in headers. Agree.

Wei Chen: Also we should avoid anything that looks like user input in telemetry. Only template metadata.

Elliot Price: +1. Docs should clearly say “template emits anonymous template/version tag; no prompts are sent as telemetry.”

Priya Shah: Please also ensure request_id shows up in logs so when users open tickets we can debug. But that’s a different KPI.

Nikhil Sharma: Ok, summarizing a decision candidate:
1) TTFS_v1 (Connectivity): start = timestamp of first template-tagged request (any HTTP status) from a given workspace/API key; success = timestamp of first subsequent template-tagged chat/generate request returning 2xx.
2) Secondary metrics: TTFS_RAG (first 2xx response from the RAG endpoint) and TTFS_Agent (first 2xx response where tool loop completes) — only if we can tag those server-side via logs/spans.

Henry Cho: I’d refine (1): success must be 2xx *and* non-empty output (tokens>0). Otherwise a 204 or weird empty response counts.

Logan Wright: We can do `completion_tokens > 0` for chat/generate responses. That’s already in usage logs.

Monica Patel: What about structured output failures where request is 2xx but parsing fails in the app? Users will still feel “not success.”

Alex Martinez: That’s why it’s v1. We can add “client_success” later based on app-level emitted event. But keep KPI anchored to server-side truth.

Talia Benson: Action items:
- Logan/Nikhil: confirm telemetry event names + how to compute TTFS in dashboard query.
- Monica/Dana: ensure template always adds template tag in requests (opt-in if header-proxy issues persist).
- Elliot/Wei: update docs language around “first success” step and what it means.

Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Logan, can you propose event schema? Something like:
- `template_request_seen` (server-side, derived from logs)
- `template_request_succeeded`
…or do we compute directly from request logs?

Logan Wright: Prefer compute directly from request logs (less instrumentation). But for the KPI dashboard, we can define derived events:
- `qs_template.first_attempt_ts` = min(ts where template_tag present)
- `qs_template.first_success_ts` = min(ts where template_tag present AND route in {chat, responses} AND status=2xx AND completion_tokens>0)
TTFS = diff.

Henry Cho: :thumbsup: Works for me.

Monica Patel: One more: If template tagging becomes opt-in due to proxies, we’ll bias TTFS towards more technical users. Can we do a “best effort” fallback? Maybe user agent contains `redwood-quickstarts`?

Logan Wright: Yes, SDK user-agent is usually allowed. We can match `User-Agent` prefix `redwood-sdk/* quickstart/*` without extra headers. Lower fidelity but better than nothing.

Nikhil Sharma: Great. Let’s lock this for PM-912 and update the doc. I’ll post a snippet in the ticket.

Elliot Price: I’ll make sure the docs call the first step “Connectivity check (counts as first success)” and then “RAG success” as the next milestone.

Priya Shah: Please include in troubleshooting: if they can’t reach TTFS due to 401/429, what to do. That’ll reduce tickets.

Alex Martinez: 👍 ship it. Keep the v1 definition simple and consistent; we can add a richer “first value” metric post-launch.
text:
Nikhil Sharma: We need to lock a definition for “time-to-first-success” (TTFS) for Quickstart Templates v1. Otherwise launch KPI dashboard is going to be bikeshedding forever. What’s the *first success*?

Henry Cho: +1. Also needs to be something we can measure reliably from telemetry (not “user felt happy”).

Talia Benson: Can we list candidate definitions + what events we can actually emit? Then pick one for v1 + a stretch metric.

Monica Patel: From DevEx POV “success” should map to the user seeing something *work* locally: they scaffold template, set env vars, run, get a response. If we make it “deploy to prod” we’ll miss most PLG users.

Elliot Price: Agree. Docs framing is “get to first working request” then “deploy + observe” as next steps. TTFS should align with the first milestone.

Wei Chen: Users in interviews basically said “if I can ask one question and it returns an answer, I’m unblocked.” They don’t think in terms of traces/metrics until later.

Logan Wright: Instrumentation reality check: We can tag template-originated traffic (header + SDK metadata) and log backend events for request success/failure. CLI can emit client-side events too, but those don’t always make it (ad blockers, offline, corporate restrictions). Server-side signal is most robust.

Nikhil Sharma: Candidate A: TTFS = time from first template init -> first successful LLM response (HTTP 200) with template tag.

Henry Cho: What about people who don’t use CLI (GitHub template) and copy/paste? If the template tag only shows up with our instrumentation, we might undercount.

Monica Patel: The tag should be in the template code path regardless of CLI. CLI is optional. For GitHub template, it’s still our template—same middleware adds header / request metadata.

Logan Wright: Yeah: template code can include `X-Redwood-Template: rag-agent@<version>` (or similar) and SDK adds `client.user_agent` / metadata. But note: proxy issues with unknown headers came up in another thread. We may need it opt-in or use query param? (also gross)

Nikhil Sharma: Right, but for KPI we can fallback to “SDK app_name field” or a low-risk header allowlist. We already have the gateway whitelist PR queued.

Priya Shah: From support side, I’d like TTFS to reflect when a user reaches a point where they would *not* file a ticket. That’s closer to “first request works” than “template cloned.” Most tickets are around auth/401, rate limits/429, streaming disconnects.

Henry Cho: So success shouldn’t be “first request attempted”. It should be “first request succeeded”. But what counts as succeeded? For chat streaming, you could get partial tokens then disconnect.

Monica Patel: For streaming, we can define success as “received at least one content token and stream ended cleanly” (finish_reason not error). But that’s harder to measure server-side.

Logan Wright: Server side we can measure: request completed with 2xx + `completion_tokens > 0` + no `stream_error` flag. We can also log a structured event when stream terminates.

Alex Martinez: Let’s keep v1 simple and robust. TTFS should be “first 2xx response from a template-tagged request” for either non-streaming or streaming. Then add a secondary “first clean streaming completion” later.

Nikhil Sharma: Ok but do we require retrieval to run? It’s “RAG + agent” template. If user runs the basic chat path without indexing anything, is that success?

Wei Chen: Template walkthrough starts with “run with sample docs” + query. But we also have a minimal “ask the assistant” endpoint for sanity check.

Elliot Price: Docs will have Step 1: “verify connectivity” (simple prompt) and Step 2: “RAG query over sample docs.” If we use Step 2 as success, TTFS will look worse.

Henry Cho: I’m worried product leadership will interpret TTFS as “time to RAG working” not “time to API works.” But we can report both.

Talia Benson: Proposal: define TTFS_v1 as *Connectivity Success*; define TTFS_RAG as *RAG Success* (secondary). Both start at the same “start clock.”

Nikhil Sharma: What’s the start clock? Signup? API key created? template init?

Logan Wright: We can do multiple start points. But cleanest PLG funnel is: `signup_completed` (backend), `api_key_created` (backend), then template events. The first template event we control reliably is “first template-tagged request seen.” But that makes TTFS always ~0.

Monica Patel: For DevEx funnel: start = when user runs `redwood-templates init` OR when app starts (first run). CLI can emit `template_init` and app can emit `template_app_start`.

Henry Cho: If CLI event is missing, we can fall back to `first_template_request_ts` and treat TTFS as undefined/missing for that user. But KPI dashboard will be messy.

Nikhil Sharma: What if start = first time we see `X-Redwood-Template` header *ever* for that user, and success = first 2xx for that same tag? Then TTFS measures “time from first attempt to first success” (still meaningful).

Priya Shah: That aligns with support: how long between “they tried” and “it worked.”

Alex Martinez: +1. That’s measurable and doesn’t depend on the CLI making it through corporate network.

Elliot Price: Works with GitHub template too. Start clock becomes “first template-tagged request (any status)”.

Henry Cho: Edge case: user’s first template request is a health check or embeddings call. Does that count?

Monica Patel: We can scope success event types. For RAG+agent template, success should be a chat/generate call from the orchestrator endpoint (not just embeddings).

Logan Wright: We can include an attribute like `template.flow = connectivity|rag|agent` and `template.endpoint = /chat` etc. But be careful about cardinality.

Nikhil Sharma: Can we hardcode a small enum? ex:
- `template_flow=connectivity` (simple “hello world” prompt)
- `template_flow=rag_query` (retrieval executed)
- `template_flow=agent_toolcall` (tool call executed)

Monica Patel: Yes. We can emit that as a low-cardinality tag on requests + in logs.

Henry Cho: How do we detect “retrieval executed” and “tool call executed”? That’s in-app logic, not gateway.

Monica Patel: Template code can set the tag when it enters those code paths. Same as we’re doing for observability spans.

Logan Wright: If it’s a request header, some proxies may block it. Alternative is to attach it as SDK metadata (body field) but that’s risky / privacy. Better: keep request header minimal (template name+version) and flow tags as internal spans/metrics.

Nikhil Sharma: Let’s not put flow tags in headers. Agree.

Wei Chen: Also we should avoid anything that looks like user input in telemetry. Only template metadata.

Elliot Price: +1. Docs should clearly say “template emits anonymous template/version tag; no prompts are sent as telemetry.”

Priya Shah: Please also ensure request_id shows up in logs so when users open tickets we can debug. But that’s a different KPI.

Nikhil Sharma: Ok, summarizing a decision candidate:
1) TTFS_v1 (Connectivity): start = timestamp of first template-tagged request (any HTTP status) from a given workspace/API key; success = timestamp of first subsequent template-tagged chat/generate request returning 2xx.
2) Secondary metrics: TTFS_RAG (first 2xx response from the RAG endpoint) and TTFS_Agent (first 2xx response where tool loop completes) — only if we can tag those server-side via logs/spans.

Henry Cho: I’d refine (1): success must be 2xx *and* non-empty output (tokens>0). Otherwise a 204 or weird empty response counts.

Logan Wright: We can do `completion_tokens > 0` for chat/generate responses. That’s already in usage logs.

Monica Patel: What about structured output failures where request is 2xx but parsing fails in the app? Users will still feel “not success.”

Alex Martinez: That’s why it’s v1. We can add “client_success” later based on app-level emitted event. But keep KPI anchored to server-side truth.

Talia Benson: Action items:
- Logan/Nikhil: confirm telemetry event names + how to compute TTFS in dashboard query.
- Monica/Dana: ensure template always adds template tag in requests (opt-in if header-proxy issues persist).
- Elliot/Wei: update docs language around “first success” step and what it means.

Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Logan, can you propose event schema? Something like:
- `template_request_seen` (server-side, derived from logs)
- `template_request_succeeded`
…or do we compute directly from request logs?

Logan Wright: Prefer compute directly from request logs (less instrumentation). But for the KPI dashboard, we can define derived events:
- `qs_template.first_attempt_ts` = min(ts where template_tag present)
- `qs_template.first_success_ts` = min(ts where template_tag present AND route in {chat, responses} AND status=2xx AND completion_tokens>0)
TTFS = diff.

Henry Cho: :thumbsup: Works for me.

Monica Patel: One more: If template tagging becomes opt-in due to proxies, we’ll bias TTFS towards more technical users. Can we do a “best effort” fallback? Maybe user agent contains `redwood-quickstarts`?

Logan Wright: Yes, SDK user-agent is usually allowed. We can match `User-Agent` prefix `redwood-sdk/* quickstart/*` without extra headers. Lower fidelity but better than nothing.

Nikhil Sharma: Great. Let’s lock this for PM-912 and update the doc. I’ll post a snippet in the ticket.

Elliot Price: I’ll make sure the docs call the first step “Connectivity check (counts as first success)” and then “RAG success” as the next milestone.

Priya Shah: Please include in troubleshooting: if they can’t reach TTFS due to 401/429, what to do. That’ll reduce tickets.

Alex Martinez: 👍 ship it. Keep the v1 definition simple and consistent; we can add a richer “first value” metric post-launch.

title: #product: 1740579013
preview:
Naomi Feldman: want to lock MVP scope for the “baseline compliance reporting pack” this week. Goal = unblock enterprise security reviews (Northbridge/Helio), not build a full GRC product. What are the *must-haves* vs can-wait?

Olga Petrov: from GRC POV, the minimum pack that passes most SOC2/ISO-ish questionnaires:
1) retention config export (by data class) + change history summary
2) audit log coverage summary (what events we emit + where they’re stored) + time-windowed export
3) data residency/region summary (where customer data/logs live)
4) access control / RBAC report (roles, permission model, who has admin)
5) evidence checklist w/ “how generated” + owner + frequency

Olga Petrov: Anything less and we’ll still be doing bespoke responses every deal.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also need clear language on “what’s NOT included” (e.g., we don’t store prompts/completions unless customer enabled logging). We keep getting tripped up by ambiguous wording.

Kevin Osei: for audit logs: we can do JSON + CSV export with filters (time window, actor, event types) in exporter. Packaging into a single zip is doable. Rendering a polished PDF is the time sink.

Naomi Feldman: agree we should not promise PDF in MVP. How do customers consume this today? SEs just attach exports + a short cover doc?

Markus Klein: in practice: procurement wants a “report” they can forward internally. But they’ll accept a zip if it’s structured + has a human-readable README. For Northbridge they explicitly asked: “audit log sample, retention policy, RBAC summary.” They did *not* ask for a custom control mapping.

Rachel Kim: +1. CS needs something repeatable. If the pack has a consistent folder structure + versioning, we can set expectation: delivered in <48h initially, later self-serve.

Ethan Park: engineering constraints:
- generating RBAC report can be cheap if we snapshot current role bindings + tenant admins.
- residency summary: we can output configured region + where telemetry is stored *by product tier*. For Dedicated/Private it’s more complicated (customer-managed pieces).
- retention config export is already planned via control plane endpoint. Change history summary is “nice” but might slip.

Marcus Lin: careful: change history isn’t just “nice” if we’re claiming auditability. But maybe MVP can be “current config + last updated timestamp + actor” vs full diff history.

Olga Petrov: yep, for MVP we can do:
- current retention settings
- last change metadata (who/when/what policy version)
Full history can be phase 2.

Kevin Osei: audit log coverage summary: do we want to include a static table of event types in the pack? If so, we need an authoritative source. Otherwise we’ll drift.

Marcus Lin: must be generated from code or exporter config. A static Confluence copy will go stale.

Ethan Park: we can include an “event catalog” artifact versioned with the exporter release (e.g., exporter emits supported event_types list). Bundle that.

Naomi Feldman: okay, so MVP deliverable = “zip bundle” with machine-readable exports + a short customer-facing README.

Naomi Feldman: Proposed folder structure:
- /README.txt (what this is, how to validate, generation time window)
- /retention/retention_config.json
- /retention/retention_last_change.json
- /audit-logs/audit_log_export.csv + .json
- /audit-logs/event_catalog.json
- /residency/residency_summary.json
- /access/rbac_summary.json
- /evidence/evidence_checklist.md
Thoughts?

Olga Petrov: looks good. README should explicitly call out:
- customer name / tenant
- report pack version
- generation timestamp
- covered period for audit log export
- contact for questions

Rachel Kim: + include “how to request” + SLA expectations (MVP = ticket to Security/GRC; later self-serve in Console).

Markus Klein: + include a one-paragraph “What this pack contains / what it does not contain” section. That helps SEs avoid over-explaining live.

Kevin Osei: for residency summary, we can include:
- control plane region
- data plane region(s)
- storage locations for logs/telemetry (at least “US/EU”)
But for Private: “customer-managed” and we can include configured values + disclaimer.

Marcus Lin: retention scope: we should list explicitly which data classes are in v1 enforcement. If traces/metrics TTL isn’t wired for all stores yet, we must say so.

Naomi Feldman: yep. MVP says “coverage” per data class: enforced / configurable / planned.

Ethan Park: re: RBAC report: do we include user emails? Might be sensitive if forwarded widely.

Marcus Lin: don’t include emails by default; include principal IDs + role names + last login maybe (if we have it). For enterprise we can add an optional “named admin list” if customer requests.

Olga Petrov: agree. Also, evidence checklist should map to control families (Access Control, Logging & Monitoring, Data Retention) but doesn’t need full SOC2 CC mapping in MVP.

Naomi Feldman: what can wait (non-MVP) so we don’t creep?

Kevin Osei: non-MVP:
- PDF rendering
- scheduled recurring delivery
- customer self-serve UI (download button) if it risks timeline
- “bring your own GRC” integrations (Vanta/Drata)

Marcus Lin: + advanced redaction / per-field policies (separate project). Also “custom report builder.”

Markus Klein: + pretty charts/screenshots. We can include links to the Console dashboards as optional.

Rachel Kim: but we should include *instructions* for where in Console to verify things (even if they can’t self-serve download yet).

Naomi Feldman: decision check:
MVP must include: retention export + last change metadata; audit log export (time-windowed) + event catalog + coverage summary; residency summary; RBAC summary; evidence checklist + README.
Not MVP: PDF, custom builders, GRC integrations, recurring delivery, full change history.

Olga Petrov: +1 :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: +1. Also please ensure “audit log coverage summary” includes statement of immutability controls / who can delete audit logs (ideally: no one; retention only via policy).

Ethan Park: we can include “deletion mechanics” section in README (purge jobs are automated; admin cannot selectively delete specific entries). Verify with Kevin.

Kevin Osei: confirmed. For MVP: we’ll state “audit log records are append-only; deletions occur only via TTL enforcement per retention policy; no per-record delete API.”

Naomi Feldman: great. I’ll update PRD + SE brief. Next: Olga to draft evidence_checklist.md template, Kevin to confirm exporter fields + event_catalog output.

Olga Petrov: I’ll post draft checklist tomorrow. Need review from Marcus for wording.

Marcus Lin: ping me when ready.

Markus Klein: once folder structure is final, SE team will make a “how to send” one-pager + template email for customers.

Rachel Kim: and CS will add it to security review playbook. Thanks all.
